itineraries.title: Classic China: Beijing, Xi'an & Shanghai
itineraries.subtitle: Three ancient capitals, from the Great Wall to the Bund's skyscrapers, via the Terracotta Warriors
itineraries.notes: Domestic flights Beijing→Xi'an→Shanghai included in the route. Spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) offer the best weather conditions. Online booking required for the Forbidden City and the Terracotta Army.

visits.text: The Forbidden City, built between 1406 and 1420 under Emperor Yongle, sprawls across 178 acres and contains 980 buildings connected by courtyards paved with 12 million bricks. Home to 24 emperors of the Ming and Qing dynasties for five centuries, it now houses the Palace Museum and its vast imperial collections—paintings, bronzes, ceramics, lacquerware. The yellow roofs—a color reserved for the emperor—crown an ensemble that's been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1987. Start at the Gate of Heavenly Peace, the monumental entrance topped with Mao Zedong's portrait since 1949, then cross through the three great ceremonial halls of the outer court.

Climb Jingshan (Coal Hill), an artificial mound rising 354 feet, built from earth excavated from the Forbidden City's moats. From the summit, you'll get a sweeping view over the entire expanse of golden palace roofs. This is where the last Ming emperor hanged himself in 1644 when an army of 400,000 peasants stormed Beijing.

activities.text: Watch the changing of the guard at Tiananmen Gate and cross the world's largest public square (109 acres).

map_keywords: Hall of Prayer for Good Harvests Beijing

visits.text: The Temple of Heaven, built in 1420 under Emperor Yongle and expanded in the 16th century under Jiajing, covers 673 acres—four times the size of the Forbidden City. The Hall of Prayer for Good Harvests, a 125-foot-tall rotunda with a 98-foot diameter, rests on 28 wooden pillars assembled without a single nail or drop of cement. The four central pillars symbolize the seasons, the next twelve represent the months. The emperor came here twice a year to pray to Heaven for bountiful harvests. Struck by lightning in 1889, the hall was rebuilt identically the following year. It's been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1998.

Step into the hutongs of the Nanluoguxiang quarter, one of Beijing's oldest alleyways with over 800 years of history. These narrow passages date back to the Yuan dynasty (1271-1368)—the word 'hutong' likely comes from the Mongolian 'hottog,' meaning 'well.' The courtyard houses (siheyuan) are arranged around small courtyards planted with pomegranate trees, a symbol of fertility. Beijing had 3,216 of them in 1944; fewer than 1,000 remain today.

activities.text: Rise early to watch local residents at the Temple of Heaven Park practicing tai chi, dancing, singing, and playing cards beneath centuries-old cypresses—an unchanging morning ritual.
Take a rickshaw ride through the Shichahai hutongs, Beijing's largest protected quarter, winding between lakes, weeping willows, and Qing dynasty mansions.

visits.text: The Mutianyu section of the Great Wall, 45 miles from Beijing, was first built in the mid-6th century under the Northern Qi dynasty, then reconstructed in granite in 1569 by General Qi Jiguang during the Ming period. The restored stretch runs 7,380 feet and features 22 watchtowers—an unusual density, with a tower roughly every 330 feet. The walls reach 23 to 26 feet in height and 13 to 16 feet in width at the top. Unique feature: crenellations line both sides of the walkway, which is exceptional—on most other sections, they only protect the outer side. The Mutianyu Pass, with its three interconnected watchtowers (Zhengguantai), forms a defensive structure found nowhere else on the Wall.

activities.text: Take the cable car up to Tower 14, then hike the ramparts between Towers 14 and 20 (western section) with panoramic views over forested hills—vegetation covers over 96% of the area. You can descend by toboggan from Tower 6, a slide that winds through the forest.

practical_info.duration: ~2.5 hours (cable car up + walk + toboggan down)

visits.text: The Summer Palace, designed in 1750 by Emperor Qianlong to celebrate his mother's 60th birthday, covers 716 acres—three-quarters of which are occupied by Kunming Lake. The emperor had 100,000 workers excavate and enlarge the lake, using the earth to raise Longevity Hill. Destroyed in 1860 by Franco-British troops during the Second Opium War, it was rebuilt in 1886 by Empress Cixi—who diverted funds meant for naval modernization. The Long Corridor, a covered gallery stretching 2,388 feet, is adorned with thousands of paintings on wood depicting landscapes, flowers, and scenes from Chinese literature. At the lake's edge, Cixi's marble boat, 118 feet long, remains moored on the north shore. It's been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1998.

The 798 Art District occupies a former industrial complex of 6.9 million square feet built in the 1950s by East German architects in Bauhaus style. Factory 718, intended for manufacturing military electronic components, was divided into six units in the 1960s—including Unit 798, which gave the district its name. After subsidies dried up in the early 1990s, the large workshops with high ceilings and north-facing skylights attracted artists drawn by cheap rents. Today there are over 200 galleries, including UCCA (107,000 square feet of exhibition space) and Tang Contemporary Art.

activities.text: Cross the Seventeen-Arch Bridge, built in 1750 under Qianlong: 490 feet long, it links the lakeshore to Nanhu Island and bears over 500 carved stone lions, each one different. From the bridge's midpoint, you can count nine arches on each side—the number nine symbolized imperial power.

visits.text: Flight from Beijing to Xi'an (about 2 hours). The Terracotta Army, discovered on March 29, 1974, by farmers digging a well, lies 19 miles east of Xi'an. Commissioned by Emperor Qin Shi Huang—who unified China in 221 BC and launched construction of the Great Wall—this funerary army was meant to protect him in the afterlife. Pit 1, the largest (755 feet × 203 feet), contains over 6,000 warriors and horses arranged in a rectangular battle formation across 11 corridors. Each statue, standing 5.9 to 6.6 feet tall, has a unique face: expressions, hairstyles, armor, and ranks differ from soldier to soldier. The statues were fired at 1,650°F in kilns, then painted with mineral pigments to distinguish units. In total, excavations have uncovered about 8,000 warriors, 130 chariots, 520 horses, and over 10,000 bronze weapons. The site has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1987.

Pit 2, to the northeast, contains about 1,000 warriors with greater variety: crossbowmen, infantry, cavalry, and war chariots in a combined unit. Display cases let you observe details up close—seven variants of Qin armor have been identified. Pit 3, the smallest (5,380 square feet), U-shaped, holds 68 high-ranking officers and a ceremonial chariot drawn by four horses: this is the underground army's headquarters. The emperor's tomb itself, covered by a 377-foot-high mound, has never been opened—archaeologists fear the artifacts would degrade upon contact with air, as happened with the first warriors' paint when they were unearthed.

activities.text: Wander the alleys and sample local specialties
Sunset over the old town from the city walls
Visit a cave restaurant and learn to cook spicy local dishes
Watch the fountain show at the pagoda in the evening
High-speed train Xi'an → Beijing
Free stroll through downtown shopping streets

Explore Tang Paradise and Furong Lake, a vast theme park southeast of the Giant Wild Goose Pagoda. This recreated Tang dynasty garden offers lakeside walks and nightly water shows celebrating China's golden age.
Explore Xi'an's Muslim Quarter and its traditional markets.
Walk through the historic center and visit the Bell Tower and Drum Tower.

visits.text: The Xi'an City Wall, built between 1374 and 1378 under the first Ming emperor Zhu Yuanzhang, forms a rectangle with an 8.5-mile perimeter around the old town. First constructed of rammed earth mixed with lime and glutinous rice juice, then covered with bricks in 1568, it rises 39 feet high, 49 to 59 feet wide at the base, and 39 to 46 feet wide at the top. A moat 66 feet wide and 33 feet deep encircles the wall. Four monumental gates pierce the ramparts at the cardinal points; 98 watchtowers, spaced 394 feet apart—a distance calculated based on half an arrow's range—line the route. Enter through the South Gate (Yongning), the most ornately decorated, whose main tower rises 105 feet.

The Muslim Quarter (Huimin Jie), at the foot of the Drum Tower, has been home to Xi'an's Hui community since the Tang dynasty (7th century), when Xi'an—then called Chang'an—was the starting point of the Silk Road. The alleys overflow with stalls selling hand-pulled noodles (biang biang mian), grilled lamb skewers, and flatbreads (roujiamo). The Great Mosque, founded in 742 during the Tang dynasty and rebuilt in the 14th century, blends traditional Chinese architecture with Islamic elements across a 129,000-square-foot garden.

The Giant Wild Goose Pagoda (Dayan Ta), built in 652 during the Tang dynasty to house Buddhist scriptures brought from India by the monk Xuanzang after 17 years of travel. The current pagoda, renovated under Empress Wu Zetian, has 7 stories and stands 210 feet tall. It's part of an ensemble inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2014 as part of the Silk Road corridor sites.

activities.text: Bike the city walls: rentals are available at each gate. Allow 1.5 to 2 hours to complete the 8.5-mile circuit, with contrasting views of the old town inside and modern towers outside. The walkway, 39 to 46 feet wide, is perfectly flat.
Tasting in the Muslim Quarter: biang biang noodles hand-pulled before your eyes, cumin lamb skewers, pao mo lamb soup (bread crumbled into broth), sesame cakes, and fresh-pressed pomegranate juice.

visits.text: Flight from Xi'an to Shanghai (about 2.5 hours). The Bund (Waitan), an Anglo-Urdu term meaning 'muddy embankment,' stretches for one mile along the west bank of the Huangpu River. Before the British arrived in 1845, there were only muddy shores here. The Opium Wars opened the port to Westerners, and 52 buildings in various styles—Romanesque, Gothic, Neoclassical, Beaux-Arts, Art Deco—gradually lined the avenue. The most imposing is the former HSBC Building (No. 12), completed in 1923, considered at the time the most sumptuous building between the Suez Canal and the Bering Strait. Next door, the Customs House (No. 13), built in 1927, sports a clock inspired by Big Ben. Across the Bund, on the opposite bank of the river, the Pudong skyline bristles with futuristic skyscrapers.

activities.text: Twilight stroll along the Bund: when lights come on along both riverbanks, the contrast between the colonial architecture and the illuminated Pudong towers is striking.

visits.text: Yu Garden (Yuyuan), built starting in 1559 by Pan Yunduan—a high-ranking Ming dynasty official—to offer his father a peaceful retreat. Construction took nearly 20 years. Across 5 acres, the garden distills the landscape art of Jiangnan: pavilions, rockeries, lotus ponds, and winding corridors, separated by 'dragon walls' whose ridges undulate and end in a dragon's head. The Great Rockery, designed by master gardener Zhang Nanyang, is a 39-foot-high artificial mountain composed of 2,000 tons of yellow Wukang stones—it's the only surviving work by this master. In front of the entrance, the Nine-Turn Bridge leads to the Huxinting Teahouse in the middle of a pond: its zigzags are meant to block evil spirits, who can only travel in straight lines.

The former French Concession of Shanghai retains its plane trees planted in the early 20th century, Art Deco villas, and shikumen—stone-gate townhouses typical of Shanghai, which appeared in the 1860s and once represented over 60% of the city's housing stock. The Tianzifang quarter, a former residential complex of narrow lanes, has been transformed into art galleries, design studios, and cafés.

activities.text: Wander through the Yuyuan Bazaar surrounding the garden, among souvenir shops, teahouses, and xiaolongbao restaurants (soup dumplings), a Shanghai specialty.

visits.text: The Lujiazui district in Pudong, a special economic zone opened in 1990, concentrates the three towers that define Shanghai's silhouette: Jin Mao Tower (1,380 feet, 88 floors, completed 1999), Shanghai World Financial Center (1,614 feet, 101 floors, 2008), and Shanghai Tower (2,073 feet, 128 floors, 2015)—the world's second-tallest building. The latter's elevator reaches the 119th floor in 55 seconds. From the top, on a clear day, the view extends across the entire megalopolis of 26 million inhabitants.

Nanjing Road (Nanjing Lu), a 3.4-mile shopping artery, links People's Square to the Bund. The pedestrianized western section, opened in 1999, concentrates department stores and brand-name shops along a half-mile stretch. At the street's end, People's Square houses the Shanghai Museum, a building shaped like an ancient bronze vessel displaying collections of Shang and Zhou bronzes, ceramics, and calligraphy.

activities.text: Ascend to the Shanghai Tower observatory (119th floor) for a 360° panorama of the city, then walk down through the Lujiazui financial district.

visits.text: Excursion to the water town of Zhujiajiao, 29 miles west of Shanghai (about 1 hour on metro line 17). Nicknamed 'Shanghai's Venice,' this 1,700-year-old town preserves lanes and buildings from the Ming and Qing dynasties. Thirty-six stone bridges span the canals, including Fangsheng Bridge—the largest five-arch stone arch bridge in the Shanghai region, built in 1571 by monk Xingchao of Cimen Temple in memory of his mother. Rising 19 feet, it's adorned with a relief of eight dragons surrounding a pearl on the central arch and stone lions at the ends. North Street (Bei Dajie), stretching half a mile, is lined with old buildings and craft shops.

Kezhi Garden, Zhujiajiao's largest private garden, blends traditional Chinese styles with Western influences in an ensemble of pavilions, ponds, and greenery. Its five-story building topped with a pavilion is the old town's highest point.

activities.text: Traditional boat ride on the canals: boatmen in local costume glide wooden craft under stone bridges and along white houses with black-tiled roofs.
Sample local specialties: zongzi (sticky rice dumplings wrapped in bamboo leaves), braised pork trotters, and sesame cakes from the North Street stalls.

seo.h1_title: Classic China: Beijing, Xi'an & Shanghai
seo.intro_paragraph: From the Forbidden City to the Terracotta Warriors, from centuries-old hutongs to Pudong's skyscrapers: this circuit connects China's three most sought-after cities. Beijing the imperial capital, Xi'an cradle of the Silk Road, Shanghai showcase of modern China.
seo.seo_hook: Twenty-four emperors ruled behind the crimson walls of the Forbidden City. Eight thousand clay warriors have stood guard for 2,200 years in Xi'an. And on Shanghai's Bund, Art Deco façades gaze at the towers of tomorrow.
featured_list: Beijing: Forbidden City, Great Wall, and hutongs
Xi'an: Terracotta Army and Ming ramparts
Shanghai: Colonial Bund and futuristic skyline
Zhujiajiao: thousand-year-old water town

places_taglines.CN::beijing: Imperial capital of 24 emperors, from the Forbidden City to the Great Wall
places_taglines.CN::xian: Cradle of the Silk Road guarded by 8,000 clay warriors
places_taglines.CN::shanghai: Metropolis where the Bund's Art Deco façades face Pudong's towers
places_taglines.CN::zhujiajiao: Water village of 36 stone bridges, Shanghai's Venice

highlights: Forbidden City and Temple of Heaven in Beijing
Mutianyu Great Wall with toboggan descent
Xi'an's Terracotta Army, 8,000 life-size warriors
Xi'an's Ming ramparts for a bike circuit
Shanghai's Bund and the Pudong skyline
Yu Garden, a Ming dynasty landscape masterpiece
Zhujiajiao water town and its peaceful canals

itineraries.title: Karst Heartlands: Guilin, Yangshuo & Longji Rice Terraces
itineraries.subtitle: Karst peaks, Li River, bamboo rafts, and rice terraces in the heart of Guangxi

visits.text: Reed Flute Cave (Lúdíyán), 5 km northwest of downtown, takes its name from the reeds that once grew at its entrance—perfect for making bamboo flutes. Carved by karst erosion roughly 700,000 years ago, the cave extends 240 meters deep with 500 meters of galleries open to visitors. Seventy-seven ink inscriptions on the walls date back to the Tang dynasty (618–907), making this one of China's oldest documented tourist sites. The central hall, the Crystal Palace of the Dragon King, measures 93 meters wide and 18 meters high. During World War II, over a thousand Guilin residents sheltered here during Japanese bombing raids.

Elephant Trunk Hill (Xiàngbíshān), where the Taohua River meets the Li River, is Guilin's emblem. This 55-meter limestone pinnacle, formed 360 million years ago on an ancient seabed, depicts an elephant dipping its trunk into the water. Between trunk and body opens the Shuiyue Cave (Moon in the Water), 12 meters high and 9.5 meters wide—at night, its arch and reflection form a perfect circle on the river. At the summit stands Puxian Pagoda, built during the Ming dynasty (1368–1644), rising about 14 meters. Legend tells of a celestial elephant sent by the Jade Emperor who fell in love with Guilin and refused to return; enraged, the Emperor pierced it with a sword, transforming it into stone.

activities.text: Evening stroll along the Two Rivers and Four Lakes circuit (Liǎng Jiāng Sì Hú). The Sun and Moon Pagodas, rising from Shanhu Lake, light up after dark—the Sun Pagoda, cast in bronze, stands 41 meters tall across 9 stories. The route follows willow-lined and banyan-shaded banks, offering views of illuminated karst peaks in the heart of the city.

visits.text: Solitary Beauty Peak (Dúxiù Fēng) and the Jingjiang Princes' Palace occupy the exact center of Guilin. The palace was built between 1372 and 1392 for Zhu Shouqian, great-nephew of the Ming dynasty founder—its construction predates Beijing's Forbidden City. Fourteen princes across twelve generations lived here for 257 years. Under the Qing, the palace became an imperial examination center where over 590 scholars trained. The peak itself—a 66-meter limestone pillar formed 350 million years ago—bears 136 rock inscriptions on its flanks, the most famous carved over 800 years ago: "Guilin's scenery is the finest under heaven." Climb 306 steps to the summit for a 360° view over the city and Li River.

Seven Star Park (Qīxīng Gōngyuán), Guilin's largest at 120 hectares, takes its name from seven karst peaks arranged like the Big Dipper. A destination since the Sui and Tang dynasties (6th–10th century), it shelters the Seven Star Cave, whose galleries stretch over 1.5 km through Putuo Mountain—an ancient underground river roughly one million years old. Entry is through the Flower Bridge (Huā Qiáo), Guilin's oldest bridge, built during the Song dynasty (960–1279). At the foot of Crescent Mountain, the Guihai Forest of Steles assembles 218 engraved tablets from the Tang through Qing dynasties, considered China's second-largest stele collection after Xi'an.

activities.text: Tasting Guilin rice noodles (Guìlín mǐfěn) at a downtown shop. The local recipe uses a pork and beef broth slowly simmered with spices, served over fresh rice noodles with braised meat and pickled vegetables. Every neighborhood has its variant—try the smoked horse version (mǎròu mǐfěn), a local specialty since the Qin dynasty.

visits.text: Li River cruise from Guilin to Yangshuo—83 km in 4 to 5 hours of navigation through hundreds of karst pinnacles sculpted by millions of years of erosion. Departure is from Zhujiang pier, 30 minutes from downtown Guilin. The most spectacular section stretches between Yangdi and Xingping, where peaks close in on both sides of the waterway. Nine Horses Fresco Hill (Jiǔmǎ Huàshān) unfolds a cliff over 400 meters high and 200 meters wide, whose natural reliefs trace nine horses in different poses. Shortly after, the Yellow Cloth Shoal (Huángbù Tān) offers the panorama printed on the back of the 20-yuan note: seven peaks reflected in the calm water like a mirror. Arrival in Yangshuo by early afternoon.

activities.text: Wandering through West Street (Xī Jiē) in Yangshuo. This 517-meter pedestrian street, paved with marble slabs, is lined with Ming and Qing architecture converted into shops, cafés, and workshops. After dark, the atmosphere shifts with red lanterns and little street restaurants where you can taste beer fish (píjiǔ yú), Yangshuo's signature dish: Li River fish cooked in local beer sauce.

visits.text: Gongnong Bridge, at the confluence of the Yulong and Jinbao rivers, offers one of the region's finest views: bamboo rafts gliding between karst peaks whose reflections ripple on the water. It's the ideal starting point for observing the countryside at dawn or sunset.

The Big Banyan Tree (Dà Róngshù), a few kilometers south of Yangshuo on the Ten-Mile Gallery, is a thousand-year-old tree standing 17 meters tall with a trunk 7 meters around. Its age is estimated at over 1,400 years, making it contemporary with the founding of Yangshuo county. Its cascading branches cover approximately 1,300 m². Scenes from the film Liu Sanjie (1961), a Chinese cinema classic, were shot nearby.

activities.text: Bamboo raft drift down the Yulong River, a tributary of the Li. The Jima–Gongnong Bridge section (about 6 km, 90 minutes) traverses the heart of karst scenery in silence: rafts are poled by the boatman with no motor. You pass under centuries-old stone bridges, between rice paddies and water buffalo, with peaks as backdrop.

Cycling the Ten-Mile Gallery (Shílǐ Huàláng), a 10 km scenic corridor linking Yangshuo to Moon Hill. The road, closed to cars during the day, winds between rice paddies, villages, and karst peaks. You pass Butterfly Spring park, the Totem Path, and several stone bridges. Bike or e-scooter rentals are easy to arrange from Yangshuo.

visits.text: Moon Hill (Yuèliàng Shān) is a 380-meter limestone peak pierced by a natural arch roughly 50 meters high and equally wide—the remnant of an ancient cave carved by karst erosion. From the road, the opening shifts shape with your viewing angle: crescent, half-moon, full moon. A trail of 800 marble steps leads to the arch in 20 to 30 minutes. At the summit, the panoramic view embraces all of Yangshuo county and the Yulong River. Moon Hill is also a climbing mecca: American climber Todd Skinner equipped the first routes here in the early 1990s, launching sport climbing in the region.

Fuli, a thousand-year-old town 8 km east of Yangshuo on the Li River's banks, is nicknamed "the town of painted fans." A large portion of its inhabitants practice the craft of folding fans, made from local mao bamboo and rice paper, decorated with Guangxi landscapes. The main street is lined with workshops where you can paint your own fan. The town also preserves Nuo culture—ritual music and dances inherited from primitive tribes—and a temple dedicated to the goddess Mazu, brought by immigrants from Fujian and Guangdong who make up much of the population.

activities.text: Countryside hike between Fuli and Liugong village along dirt paths following the Li River. The trail crosses hamlets where traditional houses nestle beside rice paddies, pomelo orchards, and resting water buffalo. Allow about 2 hours for a relaxed walk.

visits.text: Xingping, a town founded in 265 CE, is one of the oldest settlements along the Li River, 25 km northeast of Yangshuo. Its lanes paved with blue-gray slabs are lined with white-walled houses. Precisely here, at the bend in the Li River, you'll find the panorama printed on the 20-yuan note. To reach the exact viewpoint from the bill, walk or cycle about 2 km to Yuanbao Hill.

Climb Laozhai Mountain (老寨山) from Xingping: a steep trail of about 30 minutes leads to the summit where you overlook the Li River's meander at 180°, with karst peaks aligned as backdrop. This is the region's most celebrated photographic viewpoint—the same panorama that generations of traditional Chinese painters tried to capture in ink.

activities.text: Cycling or walking in the countryside around Xingping. A dirt path follows the Li River's bank south to Yucun village (about 2 km), where you observe the 20-yuan panorama from a different angle, from the riverbank. Continuing inland, trails cross orange groves and rice paddies framed by peaks, far from tourist circuits. Allow half a day to explore at your own pace.

visits.text: The Ping'an rice terraces in the Longji massif (Dragon's Backbone), about 80 km from Guilin, spread thousands of stepped plots between 600 and 800 meters altitude. Construction of these terraces began under the Yuan dynasty (1271–1368) and concluded in the early Qing dynasty (1644–1911)—over 400 years of work by Zhuang communities. The "Nine Dragons and Five Tigers" viewpoint shows nine ridges undulating like dragons bending to drink from the Jinsha River, while five tiger-shaped rocks frame them. The second panorama, "Seven Stars Surrounding the Moon," shows seven small hills encircling a crescent-shaped rice paddy.

Ping'an Zhuang village was the first Longji terraces site opened to visitors. Its traditional wooden stilt houses, the oldest dating from the Qing dynasty, cling to the slope between terraces. The Zhuang, one of China's 55 ethnic minority groups, perpetuate their architecture, textile crafts, and mountain cuisine—taste rice cooked in bamboo tubes (zhútǒng fàn), fragrant and smoky.

activities.text: Hike from Ping'an to the old Zhuang village of Longji (Gǔzhuāng) via a flagstone trail descending through the terraces. Allow 40 minutes to 1h30 depending on pace. The ancient village preserves the region's best-kept stilt houses, some over 150 years old. The return can be made on the same path or by shuttle.

visits.text: The Jinkeng Dazhai terraces, Longji's most extensive, cover 40 to 50 km² between 300 and 1,100 meters altitude. Dazhai village is inhabited by Red Yao people, recognizable by their spectacular hairstyles—women cut their hair only once in a lifetime and coil it into buns that can exceed a meter long. Three viewpoints mark the ascent: viewpoint #2, "Thousand Layers to Heaven" (Qiāncéng Tiāntī), closest to the village, shows terraces stacked like a giant stairway; #1, "Music of West Mountain" (Xīshān Sháolè), at 1,180 meters, is the highest and offers the best sunrise view; #3, "Golden Buddha Peak" (Jīnfó Dǐng), is accessible by cable car from Dazhai's parking area.

Huangluo Yao village, below the Dazhai terraces, is listed in the Guinness Book of Records for the highest concentration of long-haired women. The Red Yao perpetuate the tradition of the "Clothes Drying Festival" (Shài Yījiē), an annual celebration where brightly embroidered costumes are displayed on wooden balconies. You'll discover embroidery techniques, indigo dyeing, and local hot pepper sauce making.

activities.text: Hike from Dazhai to Ping'an crossing through Zhongliu village—a 4 to 5 hour route through the most remote and least-visited terraces. The trail alternates between ridges and valleys, with changing views over the rice fields at each pass. Bring good shoes—the path is narrow and slippery in wet weather.

visits.text: Daxu old town (Dàxū), 15 km southeast of Guilin on the Li River's banks, is one of Guangxi's four best-preserved ancient towns. Founded in 200 BCE, it was an important trading post on the Li River. Its lanes paved with river pebbles are lined with wooden and stone houses from the Ming and Qing dynasties. The covered market, still active, sells fermented tofu, medicinal herbs, and local basketwork.

activities.text: Final shopping and tasting at Guilin's central market: Guangxi tea, Flower Bridge hot pepper sauce, osmanthus cakes (guìhuā gāo)—the osmanthus flower is Guilin's emblem, whose name literally means "osmanthus forest."

seo.h1_title: Karst Heartlands: Guilin, Yangshuo & Longji Rice Terraces
seo.intro_paragraph: Limestone peaks rising like giant brushes, the Li River winding between summits, bamboo rafts gliding in silence, rice terraces sculpted over seven centuries by the Zhuang and Yao. Guangxi unfolds the landscapes that inspired traditional Chinese painting.
seo.seo_hook: Karst peaks pierce the mist, the Li River reflects the scene from the 20-yuan note, Longji terraces change color with the seasons. In Guangxi, China looks like its own ink paintings.
featured_list: Guilin: ancient caves and urban karst peaks
Li River: 83 km cruise between limestone pinnacles
Yangshuo: bamboo rafts and countryside cycling
Longji: Zhuang and Yao rice terraces spanning centuries

places_taglines.CN::guilin: Where limestone towers rise straight from the Li River's flow
places_taglines.CN::yangshuo: Karst dreamscape explored by bamboo raft and bicycle
places_taglines.CN::longji: Seven centuries of terraces carved by Zhuang and Red Yao hands
places_taglines.CN::yangshuo::xingping: Ancient riverside town framing the 20-yuan panorama

highlights: 83 km cruise down the Li River through hundreds of karst pinnacles
Motor-free bamboo raft drift on the Yulong River
Longji rice terraces over 650 years old
Encounters with Zhuang and Red Yao minorities in their villages

itineraries.title: Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian
itineraries.subtitle: Miao and Dong villages of Guizhou, giant waterfalls, covered bridges of Guangxi, Hakka fortresses and tea mountains of Fujian
itineraries.notes: Off-the-beaten-path itinerary through China's least-visited provinces. Winding mountain roads between villages, expect 2 to 5-hour drives between each stop. Guizhou is often overcast and rainy - pack a raincoat. The Miao region celebrates over 100 festivals per year, concentrated in February-March and October-November.
specialties: Fujian Hakka cuisine (stuffed tofu, pork with mustard greens)
Wuyishan Da Hong Pao oolong tea

visits.text: The walled town of Qingyan, 29 km south of Guiyang, was built in 1378 as a Ming military outpost to control the empire's southwestern frontier. Constructed entirely from local limestone—walls, streets, houses—it sprawls across 3 km² and preserves four monumental gates rising 9.5 meters high. A rare feature in China: Buddhism, Taoism, Catholicism, and Protestantism have coexisted within its walls since a French missionary arrived in 1851.

Step through the gate of Ciyun Temple in Qingyan, where stone carvings on column bases rank among the finest in Guiyang. Wanshou Palace, a former Jiangxi merchant's trading house converted into a Taoist temple in 1778, preserves a theater stage adorned with bas-reliefs depicting historical legends.

Jiaxiu Tower, in central Guiyang, stands on a rock in the middle of the Nanming River. This three-story Ming dynasty wooden structure with upturned eaves served as a study and meditation retreat for scholars. It remains the city's most recognizable symbol.

activities.text: Street food tasting in Qingyan's alleyways: braised pork trotters in soy sauce (a local specialty since the Qing dynasty), Qingyan tofu, and rice cake porridge rank among Guizhou's most celebrated snacks.

visits.text: Huangguoshu Waterfall, on the Baishui River, anchors a cluster of 18 cascades spread across 163 km². The main waterfall measures 67 meters high and 83.3 meters wide—the largest single-drop waterfall in Asia. The 17th-century traveler Xu Xiake described it as mist bursting from rock. Behind the curtain of water, Water Curtain Cave extends 134 meters with six natural windows, five chambers, and three springs. On sunny days, a rainbow forms above Rhinoceros Pool.

Doupotang Waterfall, 1 km upstream, is the widest in the group at 105 meters. It served as the filming location for the 1986 television series "Journey to the West," where monk Tang and his disciples cross the river. Before floods, it emits a low rumble that earned it the nickname "roaring waterfall."

Tianxingqiao scenic area, 7 km downstream, covers 4.5 km² of karst landscape where stones, trees, and water form natural compositions. Silver Chain Waterfall falls in parallel threads like a pearl necklace into a turquoise pool.

activities.text: Walk through Gaodang Stone Village, 6 km from the waterfalls: houses, bridges, roofs, and kitchen utensils carved from stone by the Buyi people. The village, founded during the Ming dynasty, forms a circle crossed by a river spanned by a five-arch bridge whose reflection draws a perfect circle.

visits.text: Upper Langde Miao Village, 30 km from Kaili, is a hamlet of wooden stilted houses clinging to the mountainside. Visitors are greeted with a welcome ceremony: women in embroidered costumes offer rice wine in water buffalo horns. The village preserves songs, lusheng (bamboo mouth organ) dances, and traditional embroidery without compromising to mass tourism.

Matang Gejia Village, 19 km from Kaili, houses 400 members of an officially unrecognized Miao subgroup—they consider themselves China's "57th ethnic group." According to their legend, they descend from Hou Yi, the mythical archer who shot down nine suns. The women practice beeswax batik with unique geometric patterns.

The Qiandongnan Nationalities Museum, in central Kaili, presents costumes, silver jewelry, musical instruments, and agricultural tools of the Miao and Dong peoples across eleven halls. The silver ornament collection—crowns, breastplates, necklaces—illustrates the metal's importance in Miao culture: a complete bridal set can weigh over 10 kg.

activities.text: Batik workshop in Matang: artisans teach how to draw patterns on fabric using a stylus dipped in hot beeswax, before indigo dyeing. The complete process (drawing, soaking, rinsing, drying) takes about 2 hours.

practical_info.booking: On-site, no reservation needed

visits.text: Shiqiao Miao Village, in Danzhai County, is the last place in China where paper is made by hand using Tang dynasty (7th century) techniques. More than 60 families still practice this millennial craft: local tree bark soaked, boiled in lime, beaten into pulp, then spread on a bamboo screen. The resulting paper can be preserved for 1,500 years—the National Library and National Museum of China use it to restore ancient books and paintings. The process, inscribed on the national intangible heritage list in 2006, involves more than ten steps.

Chuandong Cave, adjacent to Shiqiao village, houses an underground paper mill fed by a spring whose slightly alkaline water is considered ideal for papermaking. Stalactites grow at an unusual rate here, giving the walls a soft texture to the touch.

activities.text: Flower paper (huacao) workshop in Shiqiao: visitors dip a screen into bark pulp, place dried flowers and leaves on the wet layer, then add a second layer of pulp. Drying takes a week—the paper is then shipped.

practical_info.booking: On-site

activities.text: Kaili Sunday Market gathers Miao villagers from the surrounding area every Sunday. Embroidered textiles, hammered silver jewelry, medicinal herbs, and sour fish soup (suantangyu) mingle in a rural fair atmosphere.

visits.text: Xijiang, the "Thousand Household Village," is the world's largest Miao village: 1,432 families, over 6,000 inhabitants of whom 99.5% are Miao. Wooden fir stilted houses climb in tiers on both sides of the Baishui River, linked by Wind and Rain Bridges. The village is divided into ten natural hamlets at the foot of Mount Leigong.

The Miao Nationality Museum, in the village, traces the history of this people through costumes, orally transmitted "old songs," and agricultural tools. Special emphasis is placed on silver jewelry making—hammering, filigree, granulation—practiced by village silversmiths for generations.

activities.text: Climb to Xijiang viewpoint in late afternoon: at dusk, thousands of lights illuminate the valley, transforming the stilted houses into a luminous amphitheater.

Miao song and dance performance on the central square (around 11:30 am and 5:00 pm). The "Miao old songs" are polyphonic vocals without choir leader or instrumental accompaniment, transmitted by word of mouth for centuries.

Long table banquet (changzhuo yan): guests sit around a table several dozen meters long. Pickled sour fish, salted vegetables, and chili-boiled chicken, accompanied by rice wine served in buffalo horns.

visits.text: Zhaoxing, according to the oral tradition of the Lu clan, was founded around 1160 during the Southern Song dynasty—over 850 years of history. It's China's largest Dong village: more than 800 families, 4,000 inhabitants. The village is divided into five quarters bearing the names of Confucian virtues—Ren (Benevolence), Yi (Righteousness), Li (Propriety), Zhi (Wisdom), Xin (Honesty). Each quarter has its own drum tower, covered bridge, and theater stage.

Zhaoxing's five drum towers, built entirely of fir wood without a single nail, measure from 14.9 to 25.9 meters. Four central columns rise to the top, twelve peripheral pillars symbolize the twelve months. Lintels bear carvings of dragons, serpents, turtles, and tigers—Dong totems. Originally, a drum at the summit served to sound the alarm in case of attack or fire.

activities.text: Dong Grand Song (Dong Da Ge) concert at the drum tower square. Inscribed on UNESCO's intangible heritage list since 2009, this multi-voice polyphonic singing is performed without choir leader, without sheet music, and without instrumental accompaniment. The melodies imitate nature's sounds—flowing water, birdsong, wind in the pines.

visits.text: Tangan village, 6 km from Zhaoxing, is an ecomuseum created jointly by China and Norway. Its 160 Dong families live in stilted houses surrounded by terraced rice paddies. The village preserves a traditional gateway, drum tower, ancient tombs, wells, rice granaries, and stone pathways—a coherent ensemble of Dong rural life.

activities.text: "Eight villages and one mountain" hike from Zhaoxing: the trail crosses Tangan, Xiage, Shangdiping, Dengjiang, Jitang, and Jilun, passing through the sacred mountain of Sa Sui, a female deity revered by the Dong. Rice terraces, bamboo forests, and wooden hamlets punctuate the route.

visits.text: Chengyang's Yongji Bridge, known as the "Wind and Rain Bridge," completed in 1912, is China's most famous Dong covered bridge. Measuring 64.4 meters long, 3.4 meters wide, and 10.6 meters high, it rests on five stone piers topped with multi-tiered wooden pavilions. The entire structure was assembled without a single nail—only through interlocking fir beams. The Sanjiang region counts more than 160 drum towers and a hundred covered bridges.

The eight villages of Chengyang (Ma'an, Pingzhai, Yanzhai, Dazhai, Pingpu, Pingtan, Jichang, Guandong) form an ensemble of stilted houses, drum towers, and water mills along the Linxi River. From the bridge, tea-planted hills, rice paddies, and water mills compose a preserved Dong rural landscape.

Excursion to Biasha Miao Village (2.5-hour drive each way from Sanjiang via Congjiang), nicknamed the "gunmen's village." The men still wear the topknot shaved on the sides, a knife at the belt, and a flintlock rifle—the only community in China authorized to carry firearms. Burials take place under a tree planted at each child's birth.

activities.text: Hike between Chengyang villages along the Linxi River and tea hills. The short circuit (Chengyang → Pingtan → Guandong) crosses rice paddies and plantations over approximately 12 km.

visits.text: Transit day between Guangxi and Fujian. From Sanjiang, drive to Guilin (about 2 hours, 130 km). Guilin → Xiamen flight (1.5 hours) then drive to Yongding tulou (3 hours), or Guilin → Longyan high-speed train (5 hours) then minibus to Yongding (1 hour). Arrival at tulou late afternoon.

The Hongkeng tulou cluster gathers Fujian's most famous earthen fortresses. Zhencheng Lou, nicknamed the "Prince of tulou," is a four-story circular structure built according to the Eight Trigrams plan. Its outer rammed-earth walls mixed with glutinous rice and brown sugar reach 1.5 meters thick at the base. The interior combines Chinese and Western elements—neoclassical columns in the courtyard testify to the influence of Hakka emigrants to Southeast Asia.

Chengqi Lou in Gaobei village is the "King of tulou": four concentric rings, 400 rooms across four floors, capable of housing 600 people from the same clan. The central courtyard shelters the ancestral temple. Each family has the same number of rooms on each level—a Hakka principle of strict equality.

In 2008, 46 tulou were inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list, including 23 in Yongding district alone. These communal dwellings, built from the 12th to the 20th century, could shelter up to 800 people behind walls 1 to 3 meters thick and up to 18 meters high. Single door, no ground-floor windows, loopholes on upper floors: fortresses against bandits.

activities.text: Overnight in an inhabited tulou, with a local family. Hakka families offer rooms on the upper floors. Dinner follows Hakka recipes: braised pork with pickled mustard greens, stuffed tofu, and local tea.

practical_info.booking: Advance reservation recommended

visits.text: The Tianluokeng cluster forms the "four dishes and one soup": four round or oval tulou surrounding a square tulou, seen from above. It was by spotting these circular shapes on satellite images in the 1960s that the CIA believed it had discovered nuclear missile silos—an anecdote systematically cited in guidebooks, probably exaggerated but now inseparable from the place.

Taxia village stretches along a stream bordered by stone bridges and 24 ten-meter-high honor poles erected to celebrate clan members who obtained academic titles. The village counts 47 tulou of varied shapes among the mountains.

Yuchanglou, in Xiaban village, is a 14th-century round tulou whose interior pillars lean up to 15 degrees without the structure having collapsed in over 700 years. This "leaning tower" of Fujian intrigues architects.

activities.text: Bike ride between tulou in Nanjing valley. Country roads cross tea plantations, groves of 13 centuries-old banyans (over 300 years old) along the river, and inhabited Hakka hamlets.

visits.text: Tianyou Peak ("Heavenly Tour") is Wuyi Mountain's iconic summit: 848 steps carved into the cliff lead to a viewpoint above the 5th and 6th bends of the Nine Bend River. The panorama over red Danxia sandstone peaks crowned with subtropical vegetation justifies the climb.

The Da Hong Pao area shelters the six mother bushes of this legendary oolong tea, clinging to the cliff of Jiulongke ravine. These plants, estimated at over 350 years old, are World Heritage listed. Since 2006, all harvesting from these mother plants has been prohibited. The Tea Trail (4.5 km) descends through rock walls where hundreds of varieties of rock tea (yancha) grow to Water Curtain Cave.

activities.text: Bamboo raft ride down the Nine Bend River, 9.5 km in about 2 hours. The raft passes between 200 to 400-meter red sandstone cliffs. The raftsman comments on rock formations and legends. The river owes its name to its nine sharp turns between the peaks.

practical_info.booking: Separate ticket from pass, reserve a time slot

visits.text: Thread of Sky (Yixiantian) is a passage between two walls over 50 meters high, so narrow that the tightest point measures only 30 centimeters. You progress sideways, glimpsing a thin line of sky above your head.

Tiger Roaring Rock (Huxiao Yan) owes its name to wind rushing into a giant cavity in the cliff. The trail climbs steeply along the wall via stairs clinging to the rock, with plunging views over the subtropical forest.

Wuyi Palace, at the mountain's foot, was an 11th-century Neo-Confucian center. Philosopher Zhu Xi developed thought here that would influence all of East Asia for centuries. The UNESCO site covers 999 km² and includes ruins of a 1st-century BC Han city.

activities.text: Tea tasting at a teahouse at the foot of Wuyi Mountain. Tea masters prepare Da Hong Pao using the gongfu method: Yixing clay teapot, water at 95-100°C, successive infusions of 10 to 15 seconds. The aromas evolve from infusion to infusion—roasted notes first, then floral and fruity. Wuyi produces over 300 varieties of rock tea.

visits.text: Gulangyu Island, 600 meters from Xiamen's shore, has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2017. The 2 km² island was an international concession from 1903 to 1943, explaining its 1,000 buildings mixing colonial, Art Deco, and traditional Chinese styles. Cars are prohibited. The island once counted up to 500 pianos in the early 20th century, earning it the nickname "Music Island."

Nanputuo Temple, at the foot of Mount Wulao, is a Buddhist monastery founded during the Tang dynasty and rebuilt under the Qing. Dedicated to Guanyin, it serves an active monastic community and houses a Buddhist school. The four main halls climb up the hillside.
Sunlight Rock (Riguang Yan) is Gulangyu's highest point at 93 meters. At the summit, a 360° panorama embraces the island, the Taiwan Strait, and Fujian's mountains.

activities.text: Ferry crossing to Gulangyu from Dongdu terminal (20 minutes). Free stroll through pedestrian alleys, between colonial facades covered with bougainvillea and local pastry shops.

visits.text: Shuzhuang Garden, created in 1913 by an exiled Taiwanese scholar, integrates the sea into its landscape composition—coastal rocks are part of the garden. The Piano Museum, within the grounds, displays over 100 antique pianos from Europe and America, from the 17th to the 20th century.

Zhongshan Road, Xiamen's main shopping artery, stretches 1.2 km under 1920s-1930s colonial-style arcades (qilou). These covered galleries, typical of southern Fujian and influenced by Southeast Asian architecture, blend European and Chinese elements across three to five stories.

activities.text: Seafood tasting at Xiamen harbor market. Fujian is renowned for oyster omelets (haolicuijian), clam vermicelli, and crab spring rolls. The market bustles in late afternoon.

seo.h1_title: Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian
seo.intro_paragraph: From the thousand-household Miao villages of Guizhou to the Hakka earthen fortresses of Fujian, passing through Dong drum towers and Wuyishan's tea mountains. An off-the-beaten-path itinerary through China's ethnic minority regions.
seo.seo_hook: Polyphonic songs echo between wooden towers, stilted houses climb hillsides in tiers, round earthen fortresses shelter entire clans. Minority China reveals itself far from major cities.
featured_list: Xijiang: world's largest Miao village
Zhaoxing: five UNESCO-listed Dong drum towers
Huangguoshu: 67-meter main waterfall
Fujian tulou: UNESCO earthen fortresses
Wuyishan: birthplace of Da Hong Pao tea

places_taglines.CN::guiyang: Gateway to Guizhou and the walled town of Qingyan built in 1378
places_taglines.CN::anshun: Where Huangguoshu plunges 67 meters through limestone cliffs
places_taglines.CN::kaili: Heart of Miao country where silver ornaments tell ancestral stories
places_taglines.CN::xijiang: A thousand wooden houses climbing the valley in luminous tiers
places_taglines.CN::zhaoxing: Five drum towers rising from mist where Dong songs echo for centuries
places_taglines.CN::sanjiang: Where covered bridges cross rivers without a single nail
places_taglines.CN::yongding-tulou: Circular earthen fortresses sheltering 800 souls behind rammed walls
places_taglines.CN::nanjing-tulou: Four dishes and one soup seen from heaven's eye
places_taglines.CN::wuyishan: Where ancient tea bushes cling to red cliffs above nine river bends
places_taglines.CN::xiamen: Colonial island of a thousand pianos floating in the Taiwan Strait

highlights: Xijiang and Biasha Miao villages - living preserved cultures
Zhaoxing drum towers and Dong Grand Song - UNESCO intangible heritage
Huangguoshu Waterfall - 67 meters high, cave behind the cascade
Yongding and Nanjing Hakka tulou - UNESCO earthen fortresses
Da Hong Pao tea at Wuyishan - mother plants estimated at 350 years
Gulangyu Island - colonial architecture and car-free streets
warnings: Winding mountain roads between Guizhou and Guangxi villages - prepare for motion sickness
Guizhou is China's rainiest province - raincoat essential
Miao villages celebrate over 100 festivals per year - check the calendar
Guizhou → Fujian transition (day 9) involves a flight from Guilin - expect a transit day
Shiqiao paper: drying takes a week, flower paper is shipped by mail

itineraries.title: Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie
itineraries.subtitle: A 20-day north-to-south odyssey from the Great Wall to the Dragon's Backbone rice terraces, from Sichuan pandas to the Avatar mountains
itineraries.notes: Transportation: High-speed rail Beijing→Datong (~2h), high-speed rail Datong→Pingyao (~3h), flight Pingyao→Chengdu, high-speed rail Chengdu→Dujiangyan (~30min), flight Chengdu→Zhangjiajie, road Zhangjiajie→Fenghuang (~4h), road Fenghuang→Longsheng (~5h), road Longsheng→Guilin (~2h), flight Guilin→Hong Kong.

visits.text: The Forbidden City, built between 1406 and 1420 under Emperor Yongle, sprawls across 72 hectares and encompasses 980 buildings connected by courtyards paved with 12 million bricks. For five centuries it served as the residence of 24 emperors from the Ming and Qing dynasties. UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1987. Begin at the Gate of Heavenly Peace, a monumental entrance crowned with Mao's portrait since 1949, then proceed through the three great ceremonial halls.

Climb Coal Hill (Jingshan), an artificial mound rising 108 meters high, constructed from earth excavated when the Forbidden City's moat was dug. From the summit, sweeping panorama over the entire expanse of golden rooftops. This is where the last Ming emperor hanged himself in 1644.

The Temple of Heaven, erected in 1420 and expanded during the 16th century, occupies 273 hectares. The Hall of Prayer for Good Harvests (38 m tall) rests on 28 pillars assembled without nails or cement. UNESCO since 1998.

activities.text: Morning observation of locals in the Temple of Heaven park: tai chi, dancing, card games beneath ancient cypresses.

visits.text: The Mutianyu section of the Great Wall, 73 km from Beijing, rebuilt in granite in 1569 by General Qi Jiguang. Twenty-two watchtowers spanning 2,250 meters, walls 7-8 m high, crenellations on both sides—an exceptional configuration. The Mutianyu Pass with its three interconnected towers (Zhengguantai) is unique along the entire Wall.

The Nanluoguxiang hutongs, over 800 years of history. Narrow passages from the Yuan dynasty (1271-1368). Courtyard houses (siheyuan) arranged around small courtyards planted with pomegranate trees. Fewer than 1,000 hutongs survive today.

activities.text: Cable car ascent to tower no. 14, hiking between towers 14 and 20, toboggan descent from tower no. 6.

visits.text: The Summer Palace, designed in 1750 by Emperor Qianlong, covers 290 hectares. Kunming Lake excavated by 100,000 laborers. Destroyed in 1860, rebuilt in 1886 by Empress Cixi. The Long Corridor (728 m), the Marble Boat (36 m). UNESCO since 1998.
The 798 Art District, a former industrial complex of 640,000 m² built in the 1950s by East German architects (Bauhaus style). Over 200 galleries today, including the UCCA (10,000 m²).

activities.text: Crossing the Seventeen-Arch Bridge (150 m, 1750), adorned with over 500 stone lions, each one different.

visits.text: High-speed train Beijing→Datong (~2h). The Yungang Grottoes, 16 km from Datong: 252 caves and 51,000 Buddhist statues carved into sandstone between 460 and 525 under the Northern Wei. The complex stretches 1 km along the cliff face of Mount Wuzhou. The five Tan Yao caves (nos. 16-20) each shelter a colossal Buddha representing a Tuoba emperor—an act of repentance following anti-Buddhist persecutions. Cave no. 20: 13.7 m Buddha in meditation. Cave no. 5: largest statue at the site (17 m). The style blends Persian, Greco-Buddhist Gandharan, Indian and Chinese influences. UNESCO since 2001.
Caves nos. 5 and 6, the best preserved. Cave no. 6 features a central pagoda adorned with scenes from the life of Buddha. After the capital relocated to Luoyang in 494, work gradually ceased.

The Hanging Monastery Xuankong, 65 km from Datong, built in 491 under the Northern Wei. Plastered 50 m high on the Jinlong canyon cliff face, opposite Mount Hengshan (sacred Taoist mountain). The structure is held by horizontal beams driven into the rock—the red wooden pillars are purely aesthetic. Forty pavilions linked by vertiginous walkways, 80 statues. The only monastery in China honoring Buddhism, Taoism and Confucianism—Sakyamuni, Lao Tzu and Confucius side by side.

The Nine Dragons Screen of Datong (1392), in front of the palace of Emperor Ming Hongwu's thirteenth son. Measuring 45.5 m long and 8 m high, covered in glazed tiles. The oldest and largest of China's three existing Nine Dragons screens.

activities.text: Ascent into the monastery via stone stairway carved into the cliff, crossing the Changxian bridge between the north and south sections—plunging view over the canyon.

visits.text: High-speed train Datong→Pingyao (~3h). Fortified city, UNESCO since 1997, China's last intact walled city. Ramparts erected in rammed earth as early as 827 BC, then reinforced in stone in 1370 under the Ming. Six kilometers in perimeter, 12 m high, 6 gates, 72 bastions. Turtle silhouette seen from the sky.
The Rishengchang Bank (1823), China's first bank. Invention of interbank transfers. Martial arts-trained escorts for money transport.
The old county yamen (26,000 m², rebuilt in 1346). Daily reenactments of ancient trials.

activities.text: Strolling the lanes parallel to Ming Qing Jie. Sample mao'erduo (Shanxi 'cat's ear' noodles).

visits.text: Shuanglin Temple, 7 km away, 1,500 years old. Over 1,000 colored clay Buddhist statues, including a 26-armed Guanyin. UNESCO with Pingyao.
The Confucius Temple, the oldest preserved in China. Dacheng Hall from 1163 (Jin dynasty).

activities.text: Sunset from the ramparts—north section, view over the rooftops and Shanxi mountains.

visits.text: Flight to Chengdu. Giant Panda Research Center, founded in 1987 (6 pandas initially), today houses over 150 giant pandas across 238 hectares (after the extension completed in 2022). The giant panda has lived on Earth for 8 million years, surviving in the wild only in Sichuan, Shaanxi and Gansu. Arrive before 10am—pandas fall asleep afterward.

activities.text: Observation of red pandas in the upper section of the park. Documentary about breeding at the center's cinema.

visits.text: Wuhou Temple, dedicated to Zhuge Liang (3rd century). Gallery of 47 terracotta statues of heroes from the Three Kingdoms.
Jinli Street (550 m), a Qing-era merchant street lined with red lanterns and Chinese shadow puppet theaters.
Wenshu Monastery, founded under the Tang, in Chengdu's Tibetan quarter—the historic gateway to Tibet.

activities.text: Sichuanese tasting: hotpot with Sichuan pepper, mapo tofu, dan dan mian. The pepper produces a 'ma' numbing sensation on the tongue.
Teahouse at People's Park: jasmine tea in gaiwan, mahjong and ear cleaning—a Sichuanese ritual.

visits.text: Excursion to Dujiangyan (60 km, 30 min by high-speed rail). Dam-free irrigation system built in 256 BC by Li Bing, governor of Sichuan under the Qin. Still operating after 2,200 years—the world's oldest large-scale hydraulic work still in service. The Yuzui levee divides the Minjiang River, the Feishayan spillway evacuates floods and silt, the Baopingkou channel regulates flow. Li Bing heated then cooled the rock to carve a 20 m channel through Mount Yulei—8 years of labor, tens of thousands of workers. UNESCO since 2000.
Erwang Temple (Temple of the Two Kings) above the dam, dedicated to Li Bing and his son. Stone statue of Li Bing excavated from the river in 1974, Han dynasty.

activities.text: Crossing the Anlan suspension bridge (500 m), one of China's five most famous ancient bridges, built from woven bamboo rope under the Song dynasty.

visits.text: Flight to Zhangjiajie. National Forest Park, China's first (1982), UNESCO since 1992, 130+ km². Over 3,000 quartzite sandstone pillars, some exceeding 200 m. The Southern Sky Column (150 m) renamed 'Avatar Hallelujah Mountain'. Bailong Elevator (2002, Guinness): 326 m in under 2 min in glass cabins.

activities.text: Golden Whip Stream hike (5.7 km, ~2h) at the foot of the pillars. Wild monkeys along the route.

visits.text: Tianzi Peak, highest point in the park. Panorama over a 'sea of pillars' to the horizon. Legend: a peasant named Xiang Dakun proclaimed himself emperor here during the Taiping Rebellion.
The Yangjiajie sector, less crowded, peaceful trails and spectacular views of pillars and forested gorges.

activities.text: Park internal shuttles—the 4-day ticket includes transportation.

visits.text: Tianmen Mountain: 7.5 km cable car (1,200 m elevation gain) soaring over the 99 hairpin bends. Natural arch 131 m × 57 m accessible by 999 steps. Coiling Dragon Cliff glass walkway at 1,400 m altitude.
Grand Canyon glass bridge (430 m long, 300 m above the void). Sways slightly during crossing—by design.

activities.text: Cable car ascent (25 min) + bus descent on the 99 bends, or vice versa.

visits.text: Drive to Fenghuang (~4h). Old town ('Phoenix'), built under the Tang along the Tuojiang River, inhabited by the Miao and Tujia. Stilt houses (diaojiaolou) in wood and bamboo assembled without nails or bolts. Ming ramparts.
Hongqiao Bridge (red lanterns, Miao shops), North Gate tower, Wanming Pagoda.

activities.text: Boat on the Tuojiang River at sunset—lanterns lit, reflections of stilt houses on the water.
Crossing on the stepping stones between the two banks.

map_keywords: Nine Dragons Five Tigers Viewpoint Longsheng

visits.text: Drive from Fenghuang to Longsheng (~5h). Longji rice terraces ('Dragon's Backbone'), 100 km north of Guilin. Sculpted by the Zhuang and Yao starting from the Yuan dynasty (13th century), 650 years of labor. 66 km², altitudes from 300 to 1,100 m. In spring the flooded terraces reflect the sky; in autumn, they turn golden.
Zhuang village of Ping'an: stilt houses, 'Nine Dragons and Five Tigers' and 'Seven Stars Around the Moon' viewpoints.

activities.text: Hike between Ping'an viewpoints (~1h30) through terraces and villages. At Huangluo village, encounter with Yao women whose hair exceeds 1.80 m in length.

visits.text: Drive Longsheng→Guilin (~2h) then Li River. The landscape of karst peaks and rice paddies appears on the reverse of the 20 yuan note—the panorama precisely at Xingping. Limestone formations sculpted by erosion over millions of years.
Xingping village: old Ming and Qing street. Climb to the summit of Laozhai Hill (20 min steep climb) for the region's most photographed panorama.

activities.text: Bamboo raft cruise (Xingping section, ~1h) between karst peaks, water buffalo and cormorant fishermen.

visits.text: Yangshuo countryside and the Yulong River (Jade Dragon). Dragon Bridge (Ming dynasty) spans the river amid rice paddies framed by karst peaks. The scenic Ten-Mile Gallery road lines up rock formations and villages.

activities.text: Bicycle ride through the Yulong rice paddies (15-20 km) between stone villages, water buffalo and karst peaks. Flat trails along the river.
Bamboo raft descent on the Yulong River—calmer than the Li River. Passing beneath ancient stone bridges.

map_keywords: Tsim Sha Tsui Promenade Hong Kong

visits.text: Flight Guilin→Hong Kong. Victoria Peak (Sky Terrace 428 m). Peak Tram (1888), climbs in 6 min. Trail of 3.5 km around the peak.
Tsim Sha Tsui Promenade, Avenue of Stars. A Symphony of Lights every evening at 8pm—over 40 illuminated buildings.

activities.text: Star Ferry (founded 1880s, company since 1898). Crossing Tsim Sha Tsui-Central in 8-10 min.

map_keywords: Tian Tan Buddha Lantau Hong Kong
Tai O Fishing Village Hong Kong

visits.text: Tian Tan Big Buddha on Lantau (1993, 34 m, 250 tonnes, 268 steps). Po Lin Monastery (1920s), vegetarian lunch of the monks.
Tai O fishing village (30 min by bus). Stilt houses, dried shrimp paste.

activities.text: Ngong Ping 360 cable car (25 min) from Tung Chung. Crystal Cabin option (glass floor).

map_keywords: Temple Street Night Market Hong Kong

visits.text: Mong Kok in Kowloon: flower, goldfish, bird markets and Ladies' Market. One of the world's most densely populated districts.
Man Mo Temple, Hollywood Road (mid-19th century). Suspended incense coils burning for weeks.
Mid-Levels Escalator (800 m, 135 m elevation, 1993). World's longest outdoor covered escalator.

activities.text: Evening at Temple Street Night Market: dim sum, wonton, egg waffle, fortune tellers, street Cantonese opera.

seo.h1_title: Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie
seo.intro_paragraph: From the Great Wall to Kowloon's markets, this journey traverses China from north to south in 20 days. Imperial Beijing, the Buddhas of Yungang, the fortified city of Pingyao, Chengdu's pandas, Li Bing's 2,200-year-old dam, Avatar's pillars at Zhangjiajie, Phoenix Town, the Dragon's Backbone rice terraces and Hong Kong's electric energy.
seo.seo_hook: Fifty-one thousand Buddhas carved into sandstone at Datong. Pingyao's bankers invented interbank transfers. Li Bing tamed the Minjiang River in 256 BC. Three thousand sandstone pillars pierce the clouds at Zhangjiajie. The Star Ferry has crossed Victoria Harbor since 1888.
featured_list: Beijing: Great Wall, Forbidden City and Summer Palace
Datong: 51,000 Buddhas of Yungang Grottoes
Pingyao: China's last intact walled city
Chengdu: Giant pandas and Dujiangyan irrigation
Zhangjiajie: Avatar mountains and glass bridge
Fenghuang: Miao and Tujia stilt house town
Longsheng: Dragon's Backbone rice terraces
Guilin & Yangshuo: Li River karsts
Hong Kong: Victoria Peak and Big Buddha

itineraries.title: Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou
itineraries.subtitle: Gelugpa monasteries, yak grasslands, Qinghai Lake, the sky-mirror of Chaka, Rebkong's thangka art, Langmusi's Tibetan villages, and Jiuzhaigou's turquoise pools
itineraries.notes: High-altitude adventure itinerary (2,600 to 3,700 m) through the Tibetan regions of Gansu, Qinghai, and northern Sichuan. Acclimatization necessary—allow 1 to 2 days before any exertion. Roads sometimes unpaved between Langmusi and Songpan. Best season: June through September (green grasslands, Tibetan festivals, migratory birds at Qinghai Lake). Avoid December-February (snowy roads, extreme cold).

visits.text: Labrang Monastery, established in 1709, ranks among the six great monasteries of the Gelugpa school (Yellow Hats) of Tibetan Buddhism and stands as the largest outside the Tibet Autonomous Region. Around 1,600 monks study here across six colleges covering philosophy, medicine, astronomy, and tantra. The Grand Prayer Hall, supported by 140 massive pillars, accommodates up to 3,000 monks for collective chanting. At its early 20th-century peak, Labrang housed nearly 4,000 monks.

The Labrang kora—the circumambulation circuit—stretches 3.5 km around the monastery, lined with over 1,700 hexagonal painted prayer wheels. This is the world's longest prayer wheel corridor. Tibetan pilgrims walk it clockwise from dawn, spinning each wheel as they pass. A complete circuit takes about an hour.

The golden Gongtang Stupa, within the monastery complex, houses relics of a revered lama. Its gilded roof gleams in the sunlight above the monastery's whitewashed roofs. From the hill behind, the panorama embraces the entire monastery, the Daxia River, and the surrounding grasslands.

activities.text: Hike up the hill south of the monastery for a sweeping view over Labrang, the golden rooftops, and the Daxia River valley. The trail starts behind the white stupa to the left of the complex and takes roughly 45 minutes uphill.

visits.text: Sangke Prairie, 14 km from Xiahe, unfolds at the foot of rolling hills at 3,000 meters elevation. Tibetan nomadic families graze yaks and sheep here. In summer, wildflowers paint the steppe in color and yurt camps welcome overnight visitors.

Ganjia Prairie, quieter than Sangke, preserves the ruins of the octagonal Bajiao fortress, a rammed-earth citadel visible from the road. Nomads here live in black yak-hair tents, raising horses and sheep as their ancestors did.

activities.text: If your visit coincides with a monastic debate day at Labrang (typically afternoons), monks square off in the courtyard, clapping their hands to punctuate philosophical arguments—a striking auditory and visual spectacle.
Sample yak yogurt at the shops along Xiahe's main street. The local yogurt, thick as custard and slightly tangy, is served in ceramic bowls with sugar or honey.

visits.text: Longwu (Rongwo) Monastery, founded in 1301, is the largest monastery in Rebkong and the third most important in the Amdo region. Originally of the Sakya tradition, it was later converted to the Gelugpa school. Around 400 monks reside here in a labyrinth of prayer halls with walls covered in murals and gilded statues.

The upper and lower Wutun villages, 10 km from Tongren, are the birthplace of Regong art—thangka painting, sculpture, embroidery, and architectural decoration inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list since 2009. Nearly every household is a workshop. Artists use mineral pigments (gold, silver, turquoise, malachite, saffron, indigo) that keep colors from fading for centuries.

The Regong Art Museum in Tongren (free admission) displays over 2,000 pieces from the 11th century to the present: thangkas, sculptures, embroideries. The collection traces the evolution of this art form, which blends Indian, Nepalese, and Chinese influences into a style unique to Amdo.

activities.text: Visit a thangka workshop in Wutun village. Master painters work with a two-hair brush for the finest details, following the strict proportions of the Buddhist "Lection of Measurement." A small thangka requires at least a month; large pieces, several years.

practical_info.booking: Ask at the entrance to Wutun Monastery

visits.text: Qinghai Lake (Kokonor in Mongolian, meaning "Blue Sea") is China's largest lake: 4,500 km² of saltwater at roughly 3,200 meters elevation, framed by four mountain ranges peaking between 3,600 and 5,200 meters. Elliptical in shape, it freezes solid in winter. Tibetan Buddhist pilgrims circumambulate the lake—360 km that takes 18 days on horseback or 23 days on foot.

The Erlangjian scenic area on the south shore offers the best lake access. A jetty extends into the turquoise water with views of the snow-capped Datong Mountains to the north. On clear days, you can make out the sand dunes of Shadao Island on the far shore. In July, fields of flowering rapeseed encircle the lake in a brilliant yellow belt.

activities.text: Bike tour along Qinghai Lake's south shore. The Tour of Qinghai Lake, a professional cycling race held each July alongside the Tour de France, popularized this route. At 3,200 meters, the effort is real, but the scenery—turquoise lake, grasslands, yaks, snowy peaks—more than compensates.

visits.text: Bird Island (Niaodao), on the lake's northwest shore, welcomes over 100,000 migratory birds each spring from Southeast Asia—bar-headed geese, whooper swans, cormorants, black-necked cranes. The island itself has been closed to visitors since 2017 to protect habitat, but shoreline observatories allow viewing of the colonies from May through July.
Sand Island (Shadao), on the northeast shore, juxtaposes sand dunes and lake in a striking contrast. The area spans 120 km² with dunes rising several dozen meters high against the turquoise water.

activities.text: Visit a Tibetan nomadic camp on the grasslands bordering the lake. Families offer yak butter tea, tsampa (roasted barley flour), and share daily pastoral life—milking yaks, making dried cheese.

visits.text: Chaka Salt Lake ("salt lake" in Tibetan), at 3,059 meters elevation, covers 105 km²—ten times the area of Hangzhou's West Lake. Its salt layer reaches 5 meters deep in places. In calm weather, the lake's surface reflects the sky and snowy Qilian Mountains like a perfect mirror, earning it the nickname "Mirror of the Sky." National Tourism Geographic Magazine ranked it among the "55 places to see in a lifetime."

The old salt railway tracks, built in the 1950s to transport production, extend into the lake. Now abandoned, they serve as a photographic promenade. A tourist train runs between different areas of the site. Salt exploitation in the region dates back over 3,000 years, to the era of the Qiang people.

activities.text: Photography at sunrise or sunset on Chaka Lake—the best times for the mirror effect are before 9am and after 5pm, when the wind is low and light rakes across the surface. Wear brightly colored clothing (red, white, yellow) for reflections. Don't walk barefoot on the salt crust (cuts).

visits.text: Kumbum Monastery (Ta'er Si), 25 km from Xining, was founded in 1583 on the birthplace of Tsongkhapa, reformer of Tibetan Buddhism and founder of the Gelugpa school. Around 600 monks reside here. The site is famous for its yak butter sculptures (created in winter when hands don't melt the butter) and its eight white stupas aligned before the main entrance.

Kumbum's main hall blends Tibetan and Chinese architecture—golden roofs, white walls, red pillars. The Golden Tree Sanctuary houses a sandalwood tree that legend says grew where Tsongkhapa was conceived, bearing an image of Buddha on each of its 100,000 leaves (hence "Kumbum"—"hundred thousand images").

activities.text: Stroll through Xining's Tibetan market: turquoise and coral jewelry, Tibetan knives, incense, tsampa, and brick-compressed tea. Xining, at 2,275 meters, serves as a supply and acclimatization city before the higher-altitude stages.

visits.text: The village of Langmusi ("Tiger Temple" in Tibetan—a tiger cave once stood at the monastery site) is split in two by a river marking the border between Gansu and Sichuan. Each province has its own monastery, school, police station, and hospital. Tibetan Buddhists and Hui Muslims coexist in this high-altitude hamlet (3,300 m) with an alpine-village feel.

Kirti Monastery, on the Sichuan side, is the larger of the two: founded in 1413, expanded in 1748, it houses around 700 monks. Its particularity is preserving the intact body of the 5th Kirti Lama, displayed every 3 to 5 days. Behind the monastery, Namo Gorge marks the source of the Bailong (White Dragon) River.

Sertri Monastery, on the Gansu side, dates from 1748. Smaller but recognizable by its golden roofs, it overlooks the town from a hill. Above, a sky burial (jhator) site is still in use—bodies of the deceased are offered to vultures according to Tibetan Buddhist tradition. The place is sacred and visitors must not attend ceremonies.

activities.text: Hike through the red gorges behind Sertri Monastery. The trail climbs through wildflower meadows toward Red Mountain (Chongpoma), a red sandstone formation visible from the entire village. At the summit, prayer flags flutter facing snowy peaks. Allow 2 hours round trip.

visits.text: Namo Gorge, at the back of Kirti Valley, shelters the source of the Bailong River in a cave lined with stalactites. The trail follows the river between moss-covered limestone walls in a primeval forest atmosphere.

activities.text: Horseback riding through the grasslands around Langmusi. Local agencies offer outings from half a day to three days through alpine pastures, nomadic camps, and remote valleys. Tibetan horses, small and sure-footed, are accustomed to steep trails.

practical_info.difficulty: Moderate - altitude 3,300 m, riding experience not required

activities.text: Meal at a Hui (Muslim) restaurant in Langmusi: hand-pulled noodles, grilled lamb skewers, flatbread. The Tibetan-Muslim coexistence gives the village a dual cuisine found nowhere else.

visits.text: Flower Lake (Huahu), in the Zoige wetlands reserve, is a shallow lake surrounded by marshy grasslands at 3,400 meters elevation. From June through August, carpets of wildflowers surround its waters. Wooden boardwalks cross the marshes without damaging them. The reserve protects one of the world's largest high-altitude wetlands.

The First Bend of the Yellow River at Tangke: the river, coming from the Tibetan Plateau, traces a monumental loop through the grassland here before turning north again. At sunset, from the viewing platform, the river gleams like a golden ribbon across the steppe. It's one of the most photographed landscapes of the eastern Tibetan Plateau.

activities.text: Wildlife watching on the Zoige grasslands: black-necked cranes (protected species), Himalayan marmots, Tibetan foxes, and herds of domestic and wild yaks. Binoculars recommended.

visits.text: Songpan, an ancient Ming-dynasty fortified town at 2,850 meters elevation, preserves three monumental gates and stone ramparts that straddle the Min River. The town was a strategic trading post on the tea route between Han China and Tibet—merchants exchanged tea, salt, horses, and furs here. The streets retain a Han, Tibetan, Qiang, and Hui mix.

The covered bridge at the North Gate spans the Min River between two watchtowers. The encircling walls, 6.2 km long, were built under Emperor Hongwu's reign (14th century) to protect this key position at the empire's edge.

activities.text: Browse Songpan's market: Tibetan medicinal herbs (including the famous caterpillar fungus—yartsa gunbu), wild honey, dried yak cheese, and Qiang products. The town serves as a base for treks to Huanglong and Jiuzhaigou.

visits.text: Huanglong ("Yellow Dragon") is a 700 km² UNESCO site in the Min Mountains. Its main attraction: 3.6 km of yellow travertine terraces formed by calcium deposits from a hot spring, creating hundreds of tiered pools filled with turquoise, blue, and green water. The Five-Color Pool, at the summit at 3,576 meters, is the trail's high point—its waters shift hue with the angle of light.

activities.text: Ascend on foot or by cable car to the summit of Huanglong (3,576 m), then descend on foot along the travertine terraces (3.6 km, roughly 3 hours). Oxygen bottles are sold at the entrance for visitors sensitive to altitude. The trail crosses conifer forest, waterfalls, and Taoist temples before reaching the pools.

visits.text: Jiuzhaigou Valley ("Valley of Nine Villages") covers 720 km² in the Min Mountains, between 2,000 and 4,764 meters elevation. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1992, it takes its name from nine Tibetan villages. Its 114 lakes in turquoise, blue, green, and violet waters owe their colors to high calcium carbonate concentration and algae. Lake bottoms are visible at depths of several meters.

Five-Flower Lake, in the Rize Valley, is considered the most beautiful: its translucent water reveals a bottom of submerged logs between travertine beaches, in gradients of turquoise and emerald. Further up, Panda Lake owes its name to the giant pandas that once came to drink here—around 20 pandas still live within the park boundaries.
Nuorilang Waterfall, 270 meters wide, is China's widest travertine waterfall. Partially damaged by the magnitude 7 earthquake of August 2017, it was restored and the park reopened in 2019.

activities.text: Shuttle and walking route through Rize Valley (right branch of the Y): the bus climbs to the primeval forest at the valley's end, then the descent is on foot along wooden boardwalks that skirt the lakes. Allow 4 to 5 hours for the complete valley.

visits.text: Long Lake (Chang Hai), in Zechawa Valley (left branch of the Y), is the largest, deepest (103 meters), and highest lake in Jiuzhaigou, at 3,150 meters. Stretching 7.5 km, surrounded by forested mountains, it has no outflow stream—water seeps through the karst bottom. Local legend speaks of a monster in its depths.

Five-Color Pond (Wucai Chi), despite its modest size, offers Jiuzhaigou's most colorful waters—blue, green, yellow, turquoise, and violet mingle according to depth, minerals, and algae. It's one of the smallest lakes but the most intense in color.

Mirror Lake (Jing Hai), in Shuzheng Valley, perfectly reflects the surrounding mountains and forests in its motionless waters. The Tibetan village of Shuzheng, one of the original nine villages, preserves traditional houses and prayer wheels.

activities.text: Route through Zechawa Valley and return via Shuzheng Valley (main trunk of the Y): Pearl Shoal's cascading waterfalls, Shuzheng Tibetan village, Rhinoceros Lake. Wooden boardwalks cross conifer forests and bamboo groves—habitat of the giant panda and Sichuan golden monkey.

visits.text: Free morning in the town of Zhangzha, at the exit of Jiuzhaigou Valley. Tibetan shops offer turquoise jewelry, incense, thangkas, and ornate knives. The food market features plateau products: matsutake mushrooms, goji berries, wild honey, and dried yak cheese.

activities.text: Transfer to Jiuzhai-Huanglong Airport (1h30 drive, altitude 3,448 m) for a flight to Chengdu (45 min), Xi'an, or Beijing. The airport, perched on a mountain, offers a final panorama over the snow-capped peaks of the Min Mountains.

practical_info.booking: Book domestic flight in advance

seo.h1_title: Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou
seo.intro_paragraph: From the monastery corridors of Labrang to the turquoise pools of Jiuzhaigou, via Rebkong's thangka ateliers, the sky-mirror lake of Chaka, and the borderland Tibetan villages of Langmusi. An adventure itinerary through accessible Chinese Tibet—no permit required.
seo.seo_hook: Prayer wheels spin along golden corridors, yaks graze beside salt lakes that mirror the sky, monks debate in monastery courtyards. The eastern Tibetan Plateau opens without special permits.
featured_list: Labrang: largest monastery outside Tibet, 1,600 monks
Qinghai Lake: China's largest lake at 3,200 m
Chaka: salt lake nicknamed the Mirror of the Sky
Langmusi: Tibetan village split by a provincial border
Jiuzhaigou: 114 turquoise lakes in a UNESCO valley

places_taglines.CN::xiahe: Where the world's longest prayer wheel corridor encircles sacred halls
places_taglines.CN::tongren-rebkong: Every household is an atelier for UNESCO-listed thangka art
places_taglines.CN::qinghai-lake: Where 100,000 migratory birds meet the endless blue
places_taglines.CN::chaka: The salt lake where heaven and earth become one
places_taglines.CN::xining: Gateway to the plateau, where butter sculptures bloom in winter
places_taglines.CN::langmusi: A village where two provinces meet and prayer flags fly above red gorges
places_taglines.CN::zoige: Where the Yellow River writes its first golden curve across the grassland
places_taglines.CN::songpan: Ming ramparts guarding the ancient tea-and-horse caravan route
places_taglines.CN::huanglong: Terraced pools of turquoise flowing down a dragon's spine
places_taglines.CN::jiuzhaigou: Where crystal lakes hold forests and sky in perfect stillness

highlights: Labrang Monastery - 1,600 monks, 3.5 km kora of prayer wheels
Rebkong's thangka art in Tongren - UNESCO workshops in every house
Qinghai Lake - 100,000 migratory birds from May through July
Chaka Salt Lake - mirror reflection of sky and mountains
Langmusi - two monasteries facing each other, Tibetan and Muslim culture
Jiuzhaigou - 114 turquoise lakes in a cradle of forest and waterfalls
