itineraries.subtitle: A complete 10-day circuit around this tropical paradise, from the Creole capital Haikou to Sanya's beaches, via space launch sites, Li villages, and the Five Finger Mountain.
itineraries.notes: Hainan is a free port: French citizens get visa-free entry for up to 30 days. Avoid typhoon season (May to October) and visit November through April (sea at 26°C, clear skies). Note: Wenchang Space Launch Center is off-limits to foreigners - use the public Yaoguang viewing platform in Dongjiao Town to watch launches instead.

visits.text: Welcome to Haikou, Hainan's capital and your gateway to the tropical island. First stop: Qilou Street in the historic quarter. Over 600 arcade houses in Sino-European style, built between 1820 and 1940 by overseas Chinese returning from Singapore, Penang, and Saigon. The Sipai Building, the oldest, dates back to the Southern Song dynasty (12th century). Baroque, Indian, Arab, and Asian architecture blended together - unique in China.

Five kilometers west of downtown, don't miss Hai Rui's Tomb, honoring a Ming dynasty mandarin born in Haikou in 1514 who became a symbol of Confucian integrity. Hai Rui dared criticize Emperor Jiajing in a famous 1566 letter, was imprisoned, then rehabilitated. Mao Zedong made him an official hero. The stone mausoleum dates from 1589, restored under the Qing and again in 1996. Quiet, shaded, rarely crowded.

Stop by the Temple of the Five Officials (Wugong Ci), 2 km southeast, honoring five mandarins exiled to Hainan under the Tang and Song dynasties for standing up to the imperial court. The current temple dates from 1889, built of red lacquered wood and framed by century-old banyans. It's the island's oldest temple. Combined with Hai Rui's Tomb, allow half a day for the historical sites.

Visit the Hainan Provincial Museum, free admission, opened in 2008. You'll find 60,000 pieces covering the island's history - shipwrecks loaded with ceramics from the Maritime Silk Road, Li and Miao minority costumes, tropical forest fauna. Good context before the rest of your journey. Closed Mondays.

activities.text: Dive into Haikou cuisine at lunch. Head to Bo'ai Street for authentic Hainan noodle soup (Hainan fen) or chicken rice (Wenchang chicken rice), the island's most iconic dish, exported all the way to Singapore. Next door, a tropical fruit market: mangosteens, rambutans, dragon fruit. Also try the iced qing bu liang tea, a mix of beans, taro, and shaved ice.

practical_info.tip: Skip restaurants with picture menus, go where locals eat

activities.text: In the evening, stroll through Qilou Old Street after dark, when the arcades light up. Craft shops, café terraces, contemporary art exhibitions in former consulates. Slow atmosphere, somewhere between Macau and Singapore. Perfect for a cocktail or iced tea on a terrace.

visits.text: Head to Shishan Volcanic Geopark, 15 km southwest of Haikou. You'll find over 40 inactive craters, including Mahushan, formed roughly 13,000 years ago - geologically recent. You can descend into the caldera via basalt stairs, among ferns and strangler figs. UNESCO Global Geopark site. Allow 2 hours on-site, early morning before the heat hits.

Don't miss Dongzhai Mangrove Reserve, east of Haikou: China's largest mangrove forest, covering over 3,300 hectares, with more than 200 species of migratory birds. Boat tour through the aerial roots, stop at the observation station. Fragile ecosystem: no touching shellfish or crabs. Very few foreign tourists, unlike the rest of the island.

Holiday Beach, Haikou's urban beach, is free and open to all. You'll find blonde sand, warm water (24-26°C in winter), coconut palms, street vendors selling fresh coconuts in the background. Not the island's most beautiful coastline - save Sanya for that - but ideal for a late afternoon rest before continuing your journey. Sunset over the Qiongzhou Strait.

activities.text: Book a Hainanese cooking class with a local family (via schools like Cookly or Haikou Cooking Class). On the menu: Wenchang chicken, three-sauce lamb, sago dessert. Wenchang chicken requires slow poaching, crispy skin and white meat, served with ginger-garlic sauce. It's Hainan's national dish and the basis for Singapore's famous Hainan Chicken Rice.

Spend the evening on Haidian Island, connected to downtown by three bridges. You'll find the student and professional quarter, with rooftop bars, food trucks, and vibrant alleys. Sample Hainan coffee (Singapore style, strong, sweet, condensed milk) and watch cruise ships enter the harbor.

visits.text: Take the express train to Wenchang (50 min from Haikou East), birthplace of the Chinese diaspora to Southeast Asia. First stop: Dongjiao coconut grove, China's largest. Over 20 km of coastline planted with coconut palms, some 30-50 years old, alongside shaded bike paths. Free entry. Rent a bike at the entrance, allow 2 hours for the ride. Caribbean atmosphere, flip-flops and fresh coconut mandatory.

Head to Longlou Town, 30 minutes by car, where the Yaoguang viewing platform lets you watch rocket launches from Wenchang Space Launch Center - off-limits to foreigners. Inaugurated in May 2025, the platform covers 19,000 m² and accommodates up to 2,000 spectators during Long March 5 and 7 liftoffs. The center, operational since 2016, is China's only coastal spaceport (just 19° north latitude, ideal for launches). Check the launch schedule before going.

Ten kilometers north, you'll find Tongguling Nature Reserve, home to "eastern Hainan's first peak," an ancient volcano collapsed into the sea. Trails through subtropical forest, sweeping views over the east coast bays. Nearby Wenchang Stone Park features spectacular marine erosion formations along 2 km of coastline. Bonus: it's one of eight recommended sites for watching official launches.

Downtown, detour to Wenchang Confucius Temple, founded under the Song and rebuilt in 1042, one of the best-preserved in southern China. You'll find red courtyards, lion statues, and red lacquered wooden gates that still host spring examination rituals today. Wenchang historically produced more imperial officials than any other Hainan city.

activities.text: Lunch on Wenchang chicken in its home village. The chicken is free-range raised in coconut groves, fed on fermented coconut and small fish - hence its particularly rich meat. A typical Tianci district restaurant (part of Wenchang) has served the dish for three generations.

If luck is with you, witness a rocket launch from Yaoguang platform. A Long March 5 liftoff - roughly 869 tons at lift-off - produces a deep rumble felt for several kilometers. White light, orange trail, vibrations in your chest according to eyewitnesses. Rare spectacle in China, not to be missed if your visit coincides with a launch.

practical_info.booking: Launch schedule on official CASC website, platform requires advance booking

visits.text: Welcome to Boao, 60 km southeast of Wenchang, transformed in two decades from a small fishing village to headquarters of the Boao Forum for Asia - the "Asian Davos" founded in 2001. You'll discover the Yongchang Pavilion conference center, designed by architect Zhang Yonghe, which hosts regional heads of state every spring. The peninsula where three rivers meet (Wanquan, Jiuqu, Longgun) before flowing into the sea offers a landscape unique in China.

Walk along Yudai Beach ("jade belt"), 5 km from Boao center, which separates the river estuary from the open sea. It's one of the world's longest narrow sand strips - 8 km long and just a few meters wide. Clear water, palm trees, few people on weekdays. The sunset walk between river and sea is stunning.

Head to Xinglong, 50 km south, a former village created in the 1950s-70s to welcome overseas Chinese repatriated from Indonesia, Vietnam, Malaysia, and Burma. Today, you'll still hear Javanese, Vietnamese, and Malay spoken in some families. The Tropical Botanical Garden, founded in 1957, houses over 1,200 plant species of Southeast Asian origin.

Head to Xinglong coffee village, planted in the 1950s by Indonesian repatriates, where Hainanese arabica robusta is cultivated. You'll see old artisans still roasting over wood fires in earthen ovens. Coffee is served very strong, sweet, sometimes with butter - a local tradition derived from Javanese cafés. Small tasting and beans to take home.

activities.text: Coffee roasting workshop in Xinglong, at a family farm. You'll see the complete process: sorting green beans, roasting over open fire, hand grinding, Singapore-style tasting (arm extended, sock filter). Allow about 90 minutes. Leave with a bag of your own roast.

Taste the Malay-Chinese meal in Xinglong, a dish unique in mainland China. On the menu: Hainanese nasi lemak, satay, sambal, coconut curry rice. This Sino-Malay Creole cuisine exists only on this narrow strip of land. A legacy of the 1960s repatriates. Ice-cold Tsingtao beer to accompany.

visits.text: Welcome to Wanning, China's surf capital. You arrive at Riyue Bay ("Sun-Moon"), a Chinese stop on the World Surf League Qualifying Series - 2025 edition December 11-17, over 160 surfers from more than ten countries. Consistent waves, sandy bottom, local surf school. Waves are best November through March, when the northeast swell is consistent.

Thirty kilometers north, you'll discover Shimei Bay ("stone beauty"), which draws a perfect crescent of white sand for 9 km. It's one of the island's most beautiful beaches, still relatively unspoiled. Several luxury hotels (Banyan Tree, Le Méridien) have placed their villas here, but beach access remains public. Turquoise water as far as the eye can see, ideal for a morning at the beach before sightseeing.

Nanwan Monkey Island, 50 km south, is home to roughly 1,500 Chinese macaques. You access it via a 2,138 m cable car over the sea, inaugurated in 1996, which was long the longest of its kind in China. The monkeys are semi-wild, very curious, even aggressive if you brandish food. Don't wear sunglasses on your head, they'll steal them. Scientific reserve since 1965.

activities.text: Take a surf lesson at Riyue Bay (2 hours, board and wetsuit provided). Several PADI-certified schools on the beach, English-speaking instructors. Beginner to intermediate level - waves break gently, perfect for learning to stand up. The best session for you is 7:30 am, before wind and crowds.

practical_info.tip: Reef-friendly sunscreen mandatory, some beaches refuse others

activities.text: Late afternoon, walk along Shimei Bay seawall at sunset. Light shifts from gold to pink in fifteen minutes, the sky reflects in the shallow water. A few small grilled fish restaurants set up tables on the sand - order local grouper with salt and lime.

visits.text: Before Sanya, stop in Lingshui for Xincun Bay. You'll see the large floating village of the Tanka - a fishing people who have lived on water for 700 years, descendants of Song dynasty coastal migrants. Houses sit on bamboo rafts moored in neighborhoods. You reach it by boat, visit fish farms, lunch with locals. Remains one of China's last authentic Tanka communities.

Sanya, the island's premier beach resort, awaits you. First discovery: Yalong Bay, considered the island's most beautiful beach - 7 km of fine white sand, translucent turquoise water up to 30 meters from shore. Dubbed "China's first bay." Luxury hotels line their villas along the waterfront, but the beach is free access. Sea at 26°C in winter, ideal for swimming.

Stroll along Sanya Bay, the urban beach in the heart of the city. 22 km of coastline lined with coconut palms, dotted with small family beaches. More lively than Yalong Bay, more accessible. Spectacular sunset over the South China Sea - light shifts from orange to mauve quickly. Drink huts and bars on the sand.

activities.text: Boat in Xincun Bay to visit the Tanka floating village. Allow 1 hour navigation, plus a stop at a floating restaurant for lunch - raw fish with chili, live shrimp, ginger clams. Served on bamboo tables directly connected to the pontoons. Fair price, around 100-150 yuan per person.

practical_info.tip: Bring a hat, the sun hits hard on the water

activities.text: Evening at Sanya night market at Diyilou ("First Market"), downtown. Skewers of grilled seafood, ginger fish, chili crabs, green mangoes with salt. Locals eat here, it's packed, lively, perfect after a long day. Ice-cold Pearl River beer at 5 yuan a large bottle.

visits.text: Head southwest of Sanya to Nanshan Cultural Park, founded in 1988 for the 2,000th anniversary of Buddhism in China. You'll find across 30 km² several Tang-style temples, gardens, statues. The Guanyin of Nanshan statue, standing on an artificial island connected to the coast by a 280 m causeway, rises 108 meters high - one of the world's tallest statues. It was inaugurated on April 24, 2005, after 6 years of construction, in the presence of 108 monks from across Asia.

The Guanyin has three faces: one looks inland to bless China, the other two look to sea to protect the world. One face holds a sutra, another a rosary, the third a lotus. Mixed metal structure coated with anti-salt fluoropolymer, roughly 2,600 tons total. Inside, in Yuantong Hall, you'll find a Guanyin statue in gold and jade inlaid with precious stones (the "small" Guanyin set with 100 kg of gold and 120 carats of diamonds). You can climb to the goddess's feet for the view.

Twenty kilometers away, you arrive at Tianya Haijiao ("Edge of Sky, Corner of Sea"), a site that owes its name to large rocks inscribed with Song dynasty calligraphy. Under the Tang, it was an exile site for disgraced officials - they said it was "the end of the world." Chinese couples come en masse to bless their love, it's become an iconic wedding site in southern China.

Luhuitou Park ("the deer turns back"), on the eponymous hill, offers the best panoramic view of Sanya Bay. The bronze deer statue, erected in 1987, stands 12 m high. Li legend tells of a hunter who chased a deer to the cliff edge - the animal stopped, turned around and transformed into a young girl, who became his wife. It's the mythical origin of the Li people.

activities.text: Vegetarian meal at Nanshan Temple, famous throughout China. Refined Buddhist cuisine, no garlic or onion, based on tofu, mushrooms, vegetables and meat imitations. Served in a large quiet hall with sea views. Allow 80-150 yuan per person, it's one of Asia's finest vegetarian meals.

Relaxing evening at a Yalong Bay hotel spa. Traditional Hainanese massage with coconut oil and local aloe, 90 minutes for 400-600 yuan depending on hotels. Most resorts accept non-guests for their spas. Take advantage to recover after this big cultural day.

visits.text: Head to Wuzhizhou Island, 30 km east of Sanya. Despite its name often mistranslated as "Monkey Island," there are no macaques here - common confusion with Nanwan, don't make that mistake. Wuzhizhou is one of the island's best diving and snorkeling spots. Visibility up to 25 m in clear weather, corals, clownfish, turtles. The island covers 1.5 km², ferry from Houhai port (10 minute crossing).

Head to Haitang Bay, northeast of Sanya, which became the island's new chic bay since 2010. You'll see the Atlantis Sanya complex (opened 2018), with its giant aquarium, water park, and 1,314 rooms. Beach stretching 22 km, still wild in places - but urbanization is advancing quickly. Worth seeing for the contrast between preserved nature and XXL hotels.

If you still have energy late in the day, climb Phoenix Mountain for the panoramic view of the three bays (Yalong, Haitang and Sanya Bay). Cable car in 8 minutes, observation platform at 480 m. It's an opportunity to locate all the beaches you've seen these two days, and see the South China Sea at a glance.

activities.text: Snorkeling or introductory dive at Wuzhizhou. Several PADI-certified centers on the island, complete equipment. Diving at -8/-12 m, 30 minutes underwater, allow 600-800 yuan with a dedicated instructor. Water at 25°C even in winter, so no need for thick wetsuit.

practical_info.booking: Book the day before via your hotel or via Trip.com

activities.text: Duty-free shopping at Sanya International Duty-Free. Hainan is China's only total free port, and mainland Chinese fly in by the planeload to buy cosmetics, perfumes, watches, alcohol. Annual quotas of 100,000 yuan duty-free per person. For foreigners, it's less advantageous than Hong Kong, but simpler if you're already here.

visits.text: Head to Wuzhishan, the "Five Finger Mountain," sacred mountain of the Li, an aboriginal people present here for over 3,000 years. You'll cross China's last primary tropical forest, in a national reserve opened in 1985. The main peak reaches 1,867 m. Marked hiking trail, rare wildlife (Hainan gibbon, among the world's most endangered primates, fewer than 40 surviving individuals).

Shuiman village, nestled in the valley at 600 m altitude, is a Li cultural center. You'll see traditional "upturned boat-shaped" houses (chuanxing wu), thatched roofs. A few rare Li grandmothers still wear ancestral facial tattoos - a practice banned since 1949, dying out with them. The last tattooed generation is now 80-95 years old.

Fifteen kilometers away, Maona village offers a dive into Li daily life: brocade weaving, drying medicinal herbs, raising black-footed pigs. Li brocade (Li jin) has 3,000 years of history. Inscribed in 2009 on the UNESCO List of Intangible Cultural Heritage in Need of Urgent Safeguarding (not the Representative List), it has since paraded at Paris Fashion Week in 2024 and 2025 thanks to Dalishen patterns and Gangong bird motifs.

Rise early for the Yahu terraced rice fields, in Maoyang township, considered Hainan's most beautiful. They climb the mountain slopes, water mirrors in spring, golden waves in autumn. More modest than Yuanyang's in Yunnan, but much more accessible and less crowded. Morning light is finest.

activities.text: Li brocade weaving lesson with a local artisan in Maona or Shuiman. You learn the basics - foot loom, shuttle, cotton threads dyed with bark and roots. Li women traditionally weave sitting on the ground. Allow 2-3 hours to make a small sample (bracelet or bookmark) that you'll take home. Fair rates, around 200-300 yuan per session.

Li meal in a Shuiman family: ant chicken (the "ant chicken" is a local breed), pandanus leaf rice, black Shanlan rice wine (sacred wine served in bamboo), wild forest vegetables. Taste the "jianlan jiu," sweet cardamom wine. A cuisine very different from mainland China.

practical_info.tip: Don't step on Li house thresholds, it's taboo

visits.text: Return to Haikou in two stages. First 80 km by road from Wuzhishan to Sanya HSR station (about 1h30), as Wuzhishan isn't served by the circular rail line. Then 1h30 express train to Haikou East, along the southeast coast. The complete Hainan loop totals 653 km - the world's only circular high-speed railway, western section completed in 2015. Views of rice paddies and coconut groves from the carriage.

Upon arrival, end your stay with a stop at Haikou International Duty-Free Shopping Complex. It's the world's largest duty-free store since opening in 2022 - 280,000 m², over 800 international brands. For foreigners, it's a chance to buy alcohol, perfumes and cosmetics tax-free before the return flight.

Before Meilan airport (25 minutes from downtown), take a final walk through Qilou arcade street, grab a last Hainan coffee on a terrace. Wenchang chicken rice, noodle soup, green mango with chili - the classics for a last meal. Meilan airport has direct connections to most major Chinese cities, plus Hong Kong, Singapore, Bangkok and Seoul.

activities.text: If your flight leaves in the evening, stop at Mission Hills Haikou, the world's largest golf complex with ten courses designed by Jack Nicklaus, Greg Norman, Annika Sörenstam and others. Greens at 600-1,200 yuan on weekdays, booking required. Spa and hot springs on-site for non-golfers.

seo.h1_title: Tropical Hainan: 10 Days Around China's Southern Island
seo.intro_paragraph: Ten days to circle Hainan, China's southernmost tropical island, a free port with visa-free entry for 30 days. From the Creole capital Haikou to Sanya's beaches, via Wenchang's space launch sites, Li minority villages in the Five Finger Mountains, and coffee cultivated by Southeast Asian repatriates in Xinglong.
seo.seo_hook: White sand beaches, 108-meter Guanyin statue, rocket launches and tattooed Li villages - China's tropical island in 10 days.
featured_list: Haikou: Qilou Street with its 600 19th-century Sino-European arcade houses
Wenchang: public platform to watch Long March 5 launches
Sanya: Nanshan Guanyin statue, 108 m high, one of the world's tallest
Wuzhishan: Li villages, UNESCO brocade and the last tattooed grandmothers

places_taglines.CN::haikou: Where tropical trade winds meet 600 Sino-European arcade houses
places_taglines.CN::wenchang: Where coconut groves frame China's coastal gateway to space
places_taglines.CN::xinglong: A pocket of Java in China, where Creole coffee meets Malay-Chinese flavors
places_taglines.CN::wanning: Where turquoise waves roll into China's premier surfing paradise
places_taglines.CN::sanya: Where a 108-meter goddess watches over crystalline bays
places_taglines.CN::wuzhishan: Where ancestral Li threads weave stories among misty peaks

highlights: The Nanshan Guanyin dominates the South China Sea from 108 meters high - it's one of the world's tallest statues, inaugurated in 2005
Hainan has the planet's only circular high-speed railway: 653 km around the island, complete circuit in 4h30
Li brocade weaving techniques, found in Wuzhishan, are UNESCO-listed since 2009 and date back 3,000 years
Haikou's Qilou Street counts over 600 Sino-European arcade houses, a legacy of early 20th-century overseas Chinese returnees

itineraries.title: Deep Yunnan - beyond the classic circuit
itineraries.subtitle: An 11-day journey through Yunnan from the Hani rice terraces to the tropical rainforests of Xishuangbanna, via Ming-dynasty towns of Jianshui and UNESCO-listed pu'er tea plantations.
itineraries.notes: Yuanyang rice terraces are flooded November-March (spectacular reflections). Xishuangbanna under monsoon June-October. Possible extension via China-Laos train from Mohan to combine with Laos travel. Private car essential throughout Xishuangbanna.

visits.text: The Shilin Stone Forest rises 90 km south of Kunming: grey-black limestone pillars reaching 30 meters, sculpted by erosion over 270 million years. UNESCO listed it in 2007 as part of the South China Karst. Allow 3 hours for the Greater and Lesser Shilin. Leave early - tour buses arrive after 10 AM.

Yuantong Temple is Kunming's oldest Buddhist temple, founded under the Tang dynasty in the 8th century. Its central pond surrounded by pavilions is peaceful on weekdays. Since 1956, a Tibetan hall and a Theravada hall have coexisted here - a rare syncretism in China.
Dianchi Lake stretches across 330 km² south of Kunming. In winter (November-March), thousands of Siberian gulls overwinter here. The lakefront is accessible by bus or bicycle from downtown.

activities.text: Wander through Wenlin Jie, Kunming's student quarter: cafés run by Yi minorities, secondhand bookstores, restaurants serving Yunnan stir-fried rice with vegetables. Perfect for a first taste of local cuisine before heading out on the road.

practical_info.tip: The best spots are in the lanes parallel to Wenlin Jie, not on the main drag.

activities.text: Try clay pot rice with chicken (汽锅鸡, qiguoji) at the covered market in Jinma Biji Fang in the old town. This steam-cooked dish is a Kunming specialty - the chicken sits on a central clay cone that channels the steam. Count on 30 to 50 RMB.

visits.text: Jianshui's Confucian Temple was founded in 1285 under the Yuan dynasty. With 114 buildings spread over 7.5 hectares, it's considered China's third-largest after those in Qufu and Beijing. Unlike those two, it still functions as a school - you'll encounter high school students in the same courtyards as tourists.

Chaoyang Gate, built in 1389 under the Ming dynasty, looks exactly like the Tiananmen tower in Beijing - it was constructed four years before it. From the top, you have a direct view down the old town's main axis. Free entry.

The Shuanglong 17-arch bridge spans Lake Yilong over 148 meters. Built in 1839, it remains Yunnan's longest multi-arch bridge. Late in the day, the raking light on the lake's reflections is particularly beautiful.

activities.text: Jianshui purple pottery is made from naturally brown-violet local clay, unglazed, dating back to the Song dynasty. Visit an active workshop in the Xichuang quarter to see throwing and sand-polishing - some offer a 2-hour introduction for 80 to 120 RMB.

practical_info.tip: The most reputable workshops are in the northern part of town, on Jianxin Street.

activities.text: Don't leave without eating Jianshui grilled tofu - sold on skewers throughout the old town, topped with green chili and salt. It's Yunnan's most famous street specialty. The best stands open from 5 PM near Chaoyang Gate.

visits.text: Tuanshan lies 13 km west of Jianshui: a village whose Ming-Qing residences have remained intact since the 17th century. The courtyard houses (siheyuan) are in excellent condition - some have been converted to guesthouses. The guided tour included with entry lasts 45 minutes.

The small Xianglu stone bridge at Tuanshan's entrance dates from the same Ming period. The water is clear and village women still come to wash laundry in the morning - a daily scene unchanged for generations.

On the way back to Jianshui in the afternoon, stop at the pottery kilns in the northern quarter. These active workshops have produced unglazed purple ceramics for centuries - wood-firing in direct-flame kilns gives pieces their characteristic metallic luster.

activities.text: Have lunch in a Tuanshan house-restaurant: residents serve Bai and Han dishes cooked over wood fires in their own kitchens. No menu - they bring you what's ready. Count on 50 to 80 RMB per person.
Evening stroll through old Jianshui: Linchuan Lu pedestrian street comes alive after 7 PM with skewer stands, mahjong players, and sesame candy vendors. Less touristy than the main street.

visits.text: Yuanyang's rice terraces cover 16,000 hectares on the flanks of Mount Ailao, shaped by the Hani people over more than 1,300 years. UNESCO listed them in 2013. The Duoyishu viewpoint, at 1,940 meters altitude, is best for sunrise: flooded terraces (November-March) transform into mirrors catching the first rays. Wake by 5:30 AM minimum.

In the afternoon, descend into an authentic Hani village - Pugao Laozhai or Azheke are the least touristy. The mushroom houses (conical thatched roofs) are still inhabited. Don't photograph without asking.

If you're there on a market day (12-day calendar, not weekly), Xinjie market gathers Hani, Yi, and Miao vendors from dawn. Embroidered textiles, medicinal plants, and live pigs mingle in a mountain bazaar atmosphere.

activities.text: Hike between the terraces from Duoyishu downslope: trails follow Hani irrigation canals (a 4,653 km network also listed). Count on 2 to 3 hours to descend to Qingkou village and climb back up by tuk-tuk.

practical_info.difficulty: Easy to moderate. Closed shoes required - trails muddy in rainy season.

activities.text: At sunset, head to Bada viewpoint, 15 km south of Xinjie: west-facing terraces glow red in the last sun. The site is less crowded than Duoyishu and offers a different angle on the terraces' curves.

visits.text: Laohuzui - Tiger's Mouth - is Yuanyang's third major viewpoint, 17 km west of Xinjie. Here terraces cascade fan-like from 1,800 meters down to the Red River valley. It's the most impressive panorama for its verticality. Best time: late afternoon.

The Hani sacred forest above the villages has been protected for centuries - the Hani believe it shelters ancestral spirits. Access is free but demands respect: no noise, no litter. The dense canopy creates a particular atmosphere.
Mengpin terraces, 8 km from Xinjie, are less visited and offer the same landscapes from a northern angle. Perfect for photography without crowds. The access road is unpaved - car or moto-taxi.

activities.text: Join a Hani street banquet (长街宴, changjieyuan) if you're there during Kuzhaza (June or July by lunar calendar): hundreds of tables join into a single line in the main lane. Off-season, some hotels offer a reduced version for groups.
Before leaving, buy Hani red rice at Xinjie market - a local variety cultivated in flooded terraces, unavailable outside the region. It keeps for 6 months.

visits.text: Transit stop: the Yuanyang→Jianshui route descends 3 hours of switchback passes from 1,800 m. Arrival early afternoon. One last pass by Chaoyang Gate at sunset - the light on Ming brick differs from morning.

activities.text: Duck noodles (鸭子米线) dinner before tomorrow morning's train to Kunming - broth simmered 8 hours, served with local rice noodles. It's Jianshui's farewell dish.

visits.text: Arrival in Kunming by train from Jianshui (2h). Cuihu Park (Green Lake) downtown is perfect for the afternoon - in winter, Siberian gulls land here and Kunming residents sell them bread. Free entry.

The Yunnan Nationalities Village (云南民族村), on Dianchi Lake's shore, presents the province's 26 ethnic minorities in recreated pavilions. Useful for understanding differences between Dai, Hani, Naxi, and Yi before meeting them in real life in Xishuangbanna.

activities.text: Dinner around Jinbi Lu street: Yunnan grilled skewers (shaokao) - lamb, fermented tofu, corn - and a glass of Lancang beer before tomorrow morning's flight to Jinghong.

visits.text: Jinghong's Tropical Botanical Garden covers 1,125 hectares and houses over 13,000 plant species. Founded in 1959, it's China's largest botanical garden. The giant fern greenhouse and the dipterocarp forest alone are worth 2 hours.

Wat Manfeilong (八角亭), 65 km from Jinghong, dates from the 13th century and remains one of Yunnan's best-preserved Theravada Buddhist sites. Nine white stupas surround a central stupa - the style is clearly Thai. Shoulders and knees covered required.

Jinghong's Mekong waterfront comes alive in the evening: strollers, Dai pétanque players, and grill vendors on the bank. Here you realize you're a few hundred kilometers from Laos - the Mekong flows directly toward Luang Prabang.

activities.text: Explore Zhuque Street night market where Dai vendors sell fried insects, palm larvae, and smoked duck with local spices. Even if you don't eat the insects, the atmosphere is that of a Southeast Asian border market.

Rent an electric bike (50 RMB/day) to ride along the Mekong to Manting Dai village, 3 km from downtown: coconut palms, golden temples, saffron-robed monks. The path follows the riverbank and is completely flat.

map_keywords: Dai Village Ban Suan Men Menghan

visits.text: Menghan (Ganlanba, 'olive plain' in Dai) is a Dai village on a Mekong meander 50 km from Jinghong. The Wat Luang in Ban Suan Men village is decorated with murals depicting the Ramayana in Dai version. The welcome is simple and warm.
Ganlanba Tropical Rainforest Park, along the Mekong, is a 400-hectare secondary forest: wild banana trees, wine palms, teak. Signs are in Chinese and Dai - plan for a guide.

If you visit in April during Dai Songkran (water festival, April 13-15), Menghan is one of the best places to experience it: the festival lasts 3 days and pirogues parade on the Mekong. Off-season, Dai dance shows are organized for groups.

activities.text: Cross the Mekong by motorized boat to reach villages on the opposite bank, inaccessible by car - count on 20 RMB round-trip. Residents still cultivate sticky rice and tobacco under conditions close to those of 50 years ago.
Lunch in a Dai stilt house: sticky rice cooked in banana leaf, pork larb, bamboo shoot soup. Xishuangbanna Dai cuisine is one of the closest to northern Thai cooking - sour, herbal, not sweet.

visits.text: The Jingmai Mountains harbor the world's largest ancient tea tree forest: 2,800 hectares of tea trees planted over a thousand years ago by the Bulang and Dai peoples. UNESCO inscribed this landscape in 2023. These tea trees grow in the shade of other trees - the agroforestry protects the soil and complexifies the tea's aromas.

The Bulang village of Manpo is partially listed to preserve its bamboo and teak architecture. Bulang women still wear traditional black embroidered clothing during festivals. Outside harvest season (March-May), access is quiet.
Menghai weekly market gathers Dai, Hani, Lahu, and Akha vendors every Sunday: pu'er tea cakes, rare spices, and medicinal herbs unavailable in Kunming. Arrive before 8 AM.

activities.text: Join a pu'er tasting at a Jingmai producer: the comparison between a young sheng and a 10-year sheng is spectacular. Some also offer manual picking in March-May. Count on 150 to 300 RMB for a serious session.

practical_info.tip: Beware of free tastings that end in hard-sell - look for producers recommended by your hotel.

activities.text: Hike through the ancient tea forest at dusk: light filters between 8 to 10-meter trees. Local guides know the reputed tea trees over 800 years old - some bear tracking numbers.

visits.text: Wangtianshu Park is the only place in China where Parashorea chinensis grows - a tree whose canopy reaches 70 meters. A 2.5 km suspended walkway runs 30 meters high between the crowns. In the morning, you're in the clouds.

Nabanhe Nature Reserve protects a tropical forest corridor between Yunnan and Laos. White-cheeked gibbons are observed at daybreak, Asian elephants, and over 500 bird species. 4×4 access only - local guides offer outings departing from Mengla.

The Mohan border crossing, 25 km from Mengla, is the northern terminus of the China-Laos train inaugurated in December 2021. If continuing to Luang Prabang, a Lao visa on arrival is available at the post.

activities.text: Dawn bird outing in Nabanhe Reserve: the great hornbill (Buceros bicornis), Asia's largest hornbill with its yellow and red casque, is present in the area. Rent binoculars in Mengla (30 RMB/day) and go with a local ornithologist guide.

Dinner in Mengla at an Akha restaurant: smoked dried pork, wild vegetables gathered from the forest, and artisanal rice wine. Akha cuisine is less known than Dai but worth the detour - look for restaurants frequented by locals.

visits.text: Return to Jinghong from Mengla (3-hour drive). Wat Pajie (勐泐大佛寺), rebuilt in 1997 on the foundations of a 14th-century Ming temple, dominates the city from a hill. The walk up takes 20 minutes and offers a view over the Mekong plain.
The Dai Ethnicity Museum in the ethnocultural park traces the history of the Möng Mao kingdom (9th-17th century) and presents ancient Dai scripts, similar to Khmer and Thai. Entry 50 RMB.

activities.text: Dai Thai massage in Jinghong before the departure flight: several salons on Menglong Lu street offer massages with local herbs (lemongrass, wild ginger) for 100 to 150 RMB per hour.

seo.h1_title: Deep Yunnan - beyond the classic circuit
seo.intro_paragraph: Yunnan hides far more than Lijiang's beaten paths. In 12 days, this itinerary climbs from the Yunnan plateau to the tropical forests of the Lao border: mirror-like Hani rice terraces at Yuanyang, Ming-era old town of Jianshui, UNESCO-listed pu'er tea plantations, and the Parashorea chinensis canopy in Xishuangbanna. A journey among 13 ethnic minorities and two radically different climate zones.
seo.seo_hook: UNESCO rice terraces, thousand-year-old pu'er tea, Theravada temples, and tropical rainforest on the Lao frontier.
featured_list: Yuanyang: 16,000 hectares of flooded Hani rice terraces, sunrise reflections over 1,300 years of terrace agriculture
Jianshui: China's third-largest Confucian temple and intact Ming old town, 145 km from Kunming by express train
Jingmai: ancient tea tree forest inscribed to UNESCO in 2023, maintained by Bulang and Dai peoples for a thousand years
Wangtianshu: suspended walkway at 30 meters in the Parashorea chinensis canopy, a species found only here in China

places_taglines.CN::kunming: Where the Spring City meets stone forests carved from primordial karst
places_taglines.CN::jianshui: A Ming dynasty masterpiece of Confucian learning and purple pottery artisans
places_taglines.CN::yuanyang: Where Hani terraces cascade like liquid mirrors down mountain slopes for 1,300 years
places_taglines.CN::jinghong: Where the Mekong whispers tales of Dai temples and tropical nights
places_taglines.CN::menghan: A Dai village caught between river meanders and the flavors of northern Thailand
places_taglines.CN::menghai: Gateway to mist-shrouded mountains where thousand-year tea trees still flourish
places_taglines.CN::mengla: Where China's last rainforest meets the road to Laos beneath 70-meter giants

highlights: Yuanyang's Hani rice terraces: 16,000 hectares of terraces inscribed to UNESCO in 2013
Jianshui: China's 3rd-largest Confucian temple, intact Ming old town 145 km from Kunming
Xishuangbanna: tropical territory on Lao border inhabited by 13 minorities including the Dai
Jingmai tea mountains: century-old tea tree forests inscribed to UNESCO in 2023
